title: V451 executes untrusted packet scripts during validation
description: The validator loads the latest V448 manifest from the wifi evidence tree, extracts `preflight_script` and `live_script`, and executes them with `bash`. The prompt probes mark success solely when the process exits with rc 2 or 3. The code records whether the expected guard text was observed, but that value is never enforced. As a result, a malicious or tampered V448 packet can point to scripts that perform arbitrary host-side side effects and then exit with the expected code. V451 will report `v451-operator-script-validation-pass` and will also unconditionally record `device_commands_executed`, `device_mutations`, and `wifi_bringup_executed` as false even though it has no way to guarantee that. This undermines the stated guardrail that V451 is a bounded fail-closed prompt validation with no device commands or mutations.

title: Unpinned boot image autodiscovery can flash attacker artifact
description: The V424 live run is an approved workflow that writes an Android boot image to /dev/block/by-name/boot. However, the code does not require the operator to explicitly select a trusted Android boot image or provide an expected digest. Instead, it scans broad globs under backups/, accepts any 4 KiB-aligned file starting with ANDROID! that lacks the native marker, sorts candidates, and flashes the first valid one. Because backups/ is gitignored and likely populated from local firmware dumps or downloaded artifacts, an attacker who can get a crafted boot.img into a matching backups/baseline_*/boot.img path can cause the tool to flash that image. The subsequent SHA/readback checks only prove that the chosen file was copied and written; they do not prove that the chosen file is a known-good stock Android boot image. This creates a supply-chain/device-integrity risk where the approved handoff can install attacker-controlled boot code. The rollback logic also aborts on several post-flash failures before restore-native is reached, which can leave the untrusted Android image installed if the malicious image prevents ADB recovery steps from completing.

title: Wi-Fi HAL runner grants CAP_SYS_MODULE to HAL process
description: The commit introduces a new `wifi-hal-composite-start-only` mode that starts `servicemanager`, `hwservicemanager`, and a vendor Wi-Fi HAL. Before `execv()` of the Wi-Fi HAL, the helper applies `apply_wifi_hal_identity_contract()`. That function includes `CAP_SYS_MODULE` in the retained capability list, marks it inheritable, applies it to effective/permitted/inheritable sets, and raises it into the ambient set. Ambient capabilities survive the subsequent `execv()` of the vendor HAL binary, so the HAL receives CAP_SYS_MODULE while running as AID_WIFI. CAP_SYS_MODULE is kernel-equivalent privilege: a compromised or malicious HAL process can call `init_module`/`finit_module`/`delete_module` to load arbitrary in-memory kernel modules or unload existing modules. This directly contradicts the V405 runner's stated boundary that module load/unload is not approved. The expected fix is to remove CAP_SYS_MODULE from the Wi-Fi HAL capability set unless absolutely required, avoid raising it ambiently, and enforce the no-module boundary with least privilege and/or seccomp/LSM controls.

title: Tampered manifests can inject approved checklist commands
description: The added host-only checklist reads V340/V349 manifests from tmp/, which is gitignored, and treats their self-reported host metadata and command strings as authoritative. The clean-tree check only verifies the current Git checkout, not the integrity of these ignored manifest files. The command validator then assumes argv[1] is the expected Python runner and does not verify argv[0] is python3, does not reject duplicate flags, and does not reject extra executable wrappers. A forged V340 manifest can therefore pass all checks with a command such as `/tmp/evil scripts/revalidation/wifi_private_property_namespace_proof.py --out-dir ... --prelive-gate-manifest ... --approval-phrase ... --allow-device-mutation --assume-yes run`; the checklist marks it ready and renders that raw command in a bash block for the operator to execute. This can turn evidence-file tampering into operator-assisted host code execution or unauthorized device-command execution during the live proof workflow.

title: Manifest-controlled preflight command execution
description: The new v340 generated preflight path reads `preflight_command` from `tmp/wifi/v340-v317-final-handoff-packet/manifest.json`, tokenizes it with `shlex.split()`, and passes the resulting argv directly to `subprocess.run()`. `shlex.split()` only parses quoting; it does not prove the command is the intended Python interpreter, the intended runner script, or the `preflight` subcommand. The refresh also runs this generated step whenever `--run-approved-preflight` is set, without first requiring that the v340 handoff step passed and produced trusted/current evidence. If an attacker can cause a stale or tampered handoff manifest to be present, or otherwise influence the manifest chain, the operator running the refresh helper can be made to execute an arbitrary host-side command. The subsequent manifest checks occur only after the command has already run, and a malicious command could also write a fake manifest at the parsed `--out-dir` to make the step appear successful.

title: Approval packet emits unpinned boot-flash command
description: The added android_capture_approval_packet.py is intended to create the final operator approval boundary before a live Android boot handoff that writes the boot partition. However, the generated LIVE_COMMAND is a hard-coded executor invocation with only approval flags; it omits the approved Android boot image path/hash and native rollback image path/hash from the v299 manifest. The packet separately records and renders recommended_android_boot_image and native_rollback_image, which can make the operator believe those exact artifacts are being approved. When the operator runs the emitted command, android_capture_handoff_execute.py calls choose_images() and re-runs discovery against the current filesystem. Discovery includes globbed backup paths and choose_android_candidate() selects a valid-looking candidate by size/path ordering, not by the v299/v300 manifest hash. An attacker or unsafe artifact source that can add or replace a valid 4K-aligned ANDROID!-magic boot image under the discovered backup paths after approval, or between evidence generation and execution, can cause the live command to flash an unapproved boot image to /dev/block/by-name/boot. This can compromise device integrity or brick the device despite the approval packet showing a different approved hash.

title: QRTR helper hash is not enforced before root execution
description: The new script is documented as a no-packet, no-daemon collector, but it executes a helper from persistent device storage using the root native-init command channel. The script captures both sha-qrtr-probe and qrtr-probe-no-send in one unconditional loop, so the helper is executed even if its SHA-256 is absent or mismatched. Later, build_inventory stores qrtr_probe_sha_ok, but build_checks does not require that value to pass; the critical qrtr-probe-no-send-connect check trusts only the helper-controlled stdout fields socket.rc, send_attempted, and connect_attempted. A malicious or replaced /cache/bin/a90_qrtr_probe can therefore run as root, send QRTR/QMI traffic or start daemons, print expected no-send values, and allow the correlator to report that guardrails were preserved. This is especially relevant because /cache/bin helper trust is an explicit persistence boundary in this project.

title: Symlinkable /tmp Wi-Fi materialization can chown paths
description: The commit adds --data-wifi-mode private-empty to a root helper. When enabled, the helper creates /tmp/a90-v231-<pid>/root/data/vendor/wifi/sockets and then chmods/chowns /data/vendor/wifi and /data/vendor/wifi/sockets within that temporary tree to system:wifi. However, the temporary base path is predictable and located under /tmp, which native init mounts as mode 1777. The mkdir_p/mkdir_one helpers treat EEXIST as success and do not lstat existing components, check ownership, reject symlinks, or create the top-level temp directory with an atomic mkdtemp-style primitive. A local lower-privileged process can pre-create /tmp/a90-v231-<future-pid>/root/data/vendor/wifi as a symlink to a sensitive real directory, causing the root helper to follow it and chmod/chown the symlink target and create/chown a sockets subdirectory there. If aimed at persistent helper locations such as /cache/bin, this can grant system:wifi write access to files/directories later trusted by PID1, enabling persistence or destructive permission changes. The underlying predictable temp directory pattern existed before, but this commit introduces new real filesystem metadata mutation through chmod/chown in that attacker-influenceable tree.

title: Unverified helper execution in v249 classifier
description: The newly added wifi_cnss_runtime_gap_classifier.py runs args.helper, defaulting to /cache/bin/a90_android_execns_probe, through the root native-init 'run' command. This helper lives on writable persistent device storage according to the project threat model. Unlike the prior v248 preflight flow, this script does not capture and compare a live sha256sum of the helper before executing it. The 'helper-sha-recorded' runtime check only verifies that the CLI argument equals DEFAULT_HELPER_SHA256, not that the file on the device has that digest. It is also not a hard check, so even changing --helper-sha256 only produces an optional warning while the classifier can still pass. If /cache/bin/a90_android_execns_probe is replaced after the v248 prerequisite manifest was generated, running this classifier will execute attacker-controlled code as root before any guard output is parsed, and may still produce a trusted-looking classification if the malicious helper emits expected marker strings.

title: Vendor export follows lib symlinks and leaks host files
description: The newly added scripts/revalidation/wifi_vendor_root_evidence_export.py walks <source-vendor-root>/lib and lib64 with Path.rglob() after only checking base.exists(). If an attacker-supplied vendor root makes lib or lib64 a symlink to an external host directory, rglob() traverses that external directory. The later validation in resolve_source_file() only lstat()s the final path component; because the symlink is an ancestor, ordinary files reached through the symlink are treated as regular in-root files. copy_plan() then opens and copies them into the private evidence output. A crafted source tree containing required bin/cnss-daemon and bin/cnss_diag stubs plus lib -> /home/victim/.ssh or another sensitive directory can cause host files to be included in the exported vendor-root bundle. This also bypasses the documented no-follow/source-root guardrails and can cause denial of service by recursively walking a large host subtree before copy limits are applied.

title: Sensitive path filter can be bypassed with // paths
description: The commit adds a broker-side denylist to prevent cat from reading /cache/native-init-tcpctl.token and runtime secret directories, including in --allow-exclusive mode. However, normalize_device_path() returns posixpath.normpath(path) unchanged for absolute paths. Python intentionally preserves exactly two leading slashes, so //cache/native-init-tcpctl.token does not compare equal to /cache/native-init-tcpctl.token and does not match the /cache/a90/runtime/ prefix. The device-side cat handler passes the string directly to open(), and Linux treats multiple leading slashes as the root directory, causing the sensitive file to be read despite the broker policy. The added tests only exercise the canonical single-slash path, so they miss this bypass. Default observe-only mode is still protected because cat was moved to operator-action, but when the broker is intentionally started with --allow-operator or --allow-exclusive, the new sensitive-path invariant is bypassable, leaking the tcpctl auth token and enabling direct tcpctl root command access if the tcpctl service is reachable.

title: V459 exposes Wi-Fi PSKs through child process environment
description: The V459 generated shell script retrieves the selected saved NetworkManager profile's SSID and PSK with `nmcli --show-secrets`, then invokes the V447 Python flow using the shared `v447_env_prefix`. For WPA2/WPA3 this prefix expands to `env "A90_WIFI_SSID=$A90_WIFI_SSID" "A90_WIFI_PSK=$A90_WIFI_PSK" ...`. At runtime the full PSK is present in the `/usr/bin/env` argv briefly and then in the long-running Python child's environment. On typical Linux systems, another process running as the same local user can read `/proc/<pid>/environ` for that child, and command-line monitoring can also catch the transient `env` invocation. This undermines the new feature's goal of avoiding terminal exposure of saved Wi-Fi secrets on shared or compromised hosts. A safer design would avoid putting PSKs in argv or process environment, for example by passing them over a private pipe/stdin or a 0600 temporary file that is unlinked promptly, with the child reading from that channel.

title: V458 max-findings can disable Wi-Fi secret leak audit
description: The V458 script exposes --max-findings as an unrestricted integer. add_finding() returns without recording a finding whenever len(findings) >= max_findings. With --max-findings 0, that condition is true before the first finding is stored, so all detected secret-like evidence is discarded. leak_audit() then sets ok to not findings, causing the run to be classified as a passing sanitized bundle with leak_findings: 0. This undermines the security gate that is intended to block sharing/review of bundles when referenced Wi-Fi evidence contains private SSIDs/PSKs. The cap should either be validated as >= 1 or the scanner should track suppressed findings separately and fail if any match was detected, even when output is capped.

title: Wi-Fi PSK exposed through generated env argv
description: The V456 generator prompts for Wi-Fi credentials and then builds shell script lines like `env "A90_WIFI_SSID=$A90_WIFI_SSID" "A90_WIFI_PSK=$A90_WIFI_PSK" python3 ...`. At runtime, the shell expands those variables before executing `/usr/bin/env`, so the real SSID and PSK become argv entries for the `env` process. Process command lines are commonly observable via `ps` or `/proc/<pid>/cmdline` by other local users on the host, creating a credential disclosure risk. The intent is to avoid exporting secrets to route/proof commands, but using `env` this way introduces a command-line exposure window. A safer pattern would pass the variables as child environment without making them argv to a separate `env` binary, for example via shell assignment directly before `python3`, or by using a small subshell/export scoped only to the V447 invocation.

title: V455 trusts unverified manifests for operator commands
description: The commit introduces scripts/revalidation/wifi_strict_postroute_semantics_v455.py. In run mode, it selects the newest V454 manifest under the configured wifi root, trusts the JSON decision/pass fields, extracts script paths and preflight_command directly from that manifest, and performs only a substring marker audit on the referenced scripts. The audit does not verify that the scripts were generated by the V454 tool, reside under the selected V454 evidence directory, are syntactically valid, or that the recommended command corresponds to the audited script. Marker strings can appear in comments. If the forged manifest has decision v454-operator-strict-postroute-packet-ready and pass true, and the referenced files contain those marker strings, classify() returns v455-strict-postroute-semantics-pass and the tool prints/writes the manifest-supplied recommended_command. In a shared lab, CI workspace, or any workflow that imports/restores tmp evidence, an attacker able to place or modify tmp/wifi artifacts can cause the operator or downstream automation to execute an arbitrary host command while V455 reports that strict semantics passed.

title: V452 cleanup proof accepts missing exposure fields
description: The V452 gate is intended to block post-live stability or server-binding work unless cleanup evidence proves there is no wlan0 IP, route, DNS, validated Wi-Fi connectivity, or global listener exposure. However, cleanup_false_keys_ok is implemented as all(not state.get(key) for key in CLEANUP_FALSE_KEYS). For a missing key, dict.get() returns None, and not None is True, so omitted evidence is accepted exactly like an explicit false observation. As a result, a malformed, schema-regressed, or crafted manifest can provide only disabled_by_status plus the high-level classification booleans and still reach post_live_ready and v452-wifi-live-cleanup-proof-pass. This violates the fail-closed cleanup proof goal and can falsely green-light later Wi-Fi stability/server-binding work without proof that network exposure was removed.

title: V450 recommends unaudited manifest commands
description: The V450 audit is intended to be the final safety gate before an operator runs generated Wi-Fi preflight/live scripts. However, it only audits the files named by `packet.preflight_script` and `packet.live_script` for permissions and marker substrings. When reporting the next action, it separately returns `packet.preflight_command` or `packet.live_command` from the manifest without checking that those commands are exactly `bash <audited script>` or otherwise tied to the audited paths. A forged or tampered V448 manifest can therefore point `preflight_script`/`live_script` at benign private files containing the required markers while setting `preflight_command` or `live_command` to an arbitrary shell command. V450 can then print and write that attacker-controlled command as the recommended safe action. This is especially risky because V450 also treats missing V449 router evidence as acceptable: if `router_decision` is absent, it still returns a passing `v450-operator-preflight-ready-run-host-preflight` decision.

title: V449 trusts synthetic handoff manifests for command routing
description: The V449 router is intended to ignore synthetic, plan, dry-run, and env-missing evidence unless explicitly requested. However, evidence_state() always loads V448 packet manifests with include_synthetic=True. A manifest placed under a matching synthetic path such as tmp/wifi/v448-operator-handoff-packet-run-synthetic-*/manifest.json can therefore be selected as the latest packet, marked as a passing V448 handoff packet, and used to populate recommended_command directly from the manifest's packet.preflight_command or packet.live_command fields. The script does not execute the command itself, but it is specifically a result router whose purpose is to tell the operator the next safe command to run; returning pass=True and printing an attacker-controlled command creates a command-recommendation injection risk in shared workspaces, copied evidence bundles, or any workflow where tmp/wifi evidence is not fully trusted.

title: V447 live timeout can interrupt rollback and misreport mutations
description: V445 is a stateful live workflow that can flash the Android boot image, reboot, perform explicit Wi-Fi connect actions, and then restore the native boot image. V447 invokes it via run_step() with timeout=args.boot_complete_timeout + 900. subprocess.run(..., timeout=...) raises TimeoutExpired and terminates the child process when the wall-clock timeout expires. The default outer timeout is not derived from the sum of V445's own step timeouts and rollback budget, so a slow or maliciously stalled device/ADB path can cause V447 to kill V445 mid-flight. This can leave the device in Android instead of native init, leave Wi-Fi enabled/connected before cleanup, or interrupt rollback after boot partition changes. The top-level V447 manifest compounds this by setting device_commands_executed, device_mutations, and wifi_bringup_executed solely from the nested V445 manifest; if V445 was killed before writing that manifest, all three fields become false even though V445 may already have mutated the device.

title: Secret guard can be bypassed with --max-findings 0
description: The V446 scanner is intended to fail closed when tracked or included-untracked repository files contain private Wi-Fi policy data or raw A90_WIFI_SSID/A90_WIFI_PSK assignments. However, --max-findings is accepted as an unconstrained integer. add_finding() refuses to append a finding whenever len(findings) >= max_findings. With --max-findings 0, that condition is true before any finding is recorded, so all detections are silently dropped. classify() then sees an empty findings list and returns v446-wifi-private-secret-guard-pass. I verified this by creating an untracked probe file containing A90_WIFI_SSID and A90_WIFI_PSK assignments: the default run failed with findings, while the same scan with --max-findings 0 returned pass=True and exit code 0. This creates a fail-open bypass for a security gate whose purpose is to prevent accidental credential exposure.

title: Wi-Fi PSK exposed in adb process command line
description: The V445 collector loads the private SSID/PSK from environment variables after approval, builds a raw `cmd wifi connect-network` shell command containing those secret values, and executes it as an argument to the `adb` process. Although the evidence display uses placeholders and shlex quoting reduces shell injection risk, the actual secret-bearing command is still present in the host-side process argv for the lifetime of the adb invocation. On a shared lab host, another local user or compromised local process can poll `/proc/*/cmdline` or `ps` output and recover the Wi-Fi PSK and SSID. This violates the stated guardrail that raw Wi-Fi identifiers and credentials are not exposed, and the issue is introduced by the new live runner.

title: Wi-Fi stability gate accepts partial exposure samples
description: V441 documents that a stable PASS must show that all V439 samples had Wi-Fi route/DNS/connectivity exposure. However, the implementation defines exposure_seen() with any(...) across multiple fields. stable_all_samples then only checks that every sample satisfies this weak predicate. As a result, a run where every sample has wifi_connected=True but no wlan0 IP, no default route, no route-get result, no validated connectivity, and no DNS surface can still be classified as v441-android-wifi-exposure-stability-cleanup-pass if listener and cleanup checks pass. This is a security-relevant validation/policy bug because this script is used as an exposure-aware gate before broader Wi-Fi credential/target and eventual service-exposure work; it can produce a false safety/stability decision from insufficient evidence.

title: Wi-Fi policy gate passes despite unsafe listener evidence
description: The V440 selector extracts `exposure_seen`, `cleanup_contained`, and `listener_safe`, but only uses `listener_safe` in the first contained-lab branch. If evidence has `exposure_seen=True`, `cleanup_contained=True`, `listener_safe=False`, and `enabled_seen=True`, the code skips both exposure branches and falls through to `v440-android-wifi-policy-extended-observation-pass`. That pass reason claims there was no active route/DNS/connectivity exposure even though `exposure_seen` is true, and it ignores the unsafe global listener condition. This undermines the policy gate meant to keep Wi-Fi/server exposure blocked until listener policy is explicit.

title: V439 passes despite observed global listeners
description: V439 is intended to observe Android Wi-Fi auto-connect without approving server exposure. The plan explicitly says server exposure or new Wi-Fi-facing listeners are not allowed. However, the new collector only computes `listener_safe` as a summary field and does not use it in the PASS decision. If any sample has `global_listener_observed=True` and Wi-Fi exposure is also seen, `classify()` still returns `pass=True` with an exposure-observed decision. If cleanup is requested and later succeeds, the sample-window listener exposure is still treated as an overall PASS; if cleanup is not requested, the same PASS can be returned while the listener remains exposed. This can cause automation or an operator to treat an externally reachable listener state as approved evidence and proceed to later gates.

title: V438 Wi-Fi gate misses IPv6 wlan0 exposure
description: The commit adds a workflow that intentionally enables Android Wi-Fi and then decides whether the device stayed contained. That decision relies on inherited captures and helper parsers that only check IPv4-oriented state: `ip route`/IPv4 `route get`, `inet` IPv4 addresses, and a dumpsys filter containing `IPv4` but not `IPv6`. The V438 classifier then treats absence of those booleans as no post-enable exposure and can return `v438-android-wifi-reenable-enabled-contained-pass`. On a network where Wi-Fi auto-connects with IPv6-only or IPv6-preferred addressing, wlan0 could receive a global IPv6 address/default route or expose IPv6-reachable listeners without setting `wlan0_has_ip`, `default_route_wlan`, or `route_get_wlan`. This undermines the safety gate and could let later workflow proceed under a false containment assumption.

title: V437 gate trusts truthy strings in Wi-Fi evidence
description: The new host-side decision gate loads the latest matching V436 manifest, or a caller-supplied manifest, without schema/type validation. It then evaluates the top-level pass field and containment markers using bool(). In Python, non-empty strings, dictionaries, and lists are truthy, so JSON values like "pass": "False" and "disabled": "False" satisfy the containment predicate. As a result, a malformed or tampered manifest can produce decision v437-wifi-branch-controlled-reenable-selected and exit successfully even though the evidence says the containment checks are false. The script itself does not mutate the device, but it is explicitly a safety gate for the next step that may enable Android Wi-Fi. Automation or an operator relying on the successful exit code/decision could proceed to a more exposed Wi-Fi state based on invalid evidence.

title: V436 Wi-Fi check treats missing evidence as safe
description: The V436 collector classifies safety with expressions such as `not sample.get("default_route_wlan")` and `not sample.get("wlan0_has_ip")`. That means an absent key, empty parser result, or failed capture is treated the same as verified absence of exposure. `run_capture_mode()` runs the capture set and immediately calls `phase_state()`/`classify()` without requiring each required capture to succeed or produce trustworthy evidence. As a result, if Android/ADB output is incomplete or a compromised/misbehaving device causes the route, netdev, connectivity, or listener commands to return empty/failing output, the checker can still return `v436-android-wifi-disabled-persistence-pass` as long as Wi-Fi status/settings appear disabled. This is a verification-bypass bug: it can falsely certify that Wi-Fi/network exposure is absent, undermining the containment gate used before later device-security work.

title: Brittle Wi-Fi containment classifier can falsely pass
description: The V435 collector narrows active Wi-Fi connectivity detection to lines containing both `NetworkAgentInfo{` and the literal substring `wifi connected`, and narrows DNS detection to a single `NetworkAgentInfo{` line that also contains `wlan0` and DNS markers. Android `dumpsys connectivity` commonly represents active Wi-Fi as `type: WIFI[], state: CONNECTED/CONNECTED`, or splits NetworkAgentInfo, NetworkCapabilities, NetworkInfo, LinkProperties, routes, interface and DNS data across multiple filtered lines. Those formats are not matched. The classifier then treats `connectivity_gone` as true when these two brittle detectors return false, and can return `v435-android-wifi-autoconnect-contained-pass` if the Wi-Fi setting reports disabled and route/listener checks do not catch the remaining exposure. Because the handoff wrapper trusts the nested collector pass result, later automation may proceed under the false assumption that Wi-Fi route/DNS/connectivity exposure has been removed, potentially enabling unsafe server-exposure gates in a lab where Android Wi-Fi is still reachable.

title: V434 policy can pass on incomplete V433 evidence
description: The V434 selector loads the latest matching V433 manifest, or an explicitly supplied one, but does not validate that it is a real V433 run result, that it contains the expected comparison/classification state, or that wifi_bringup_executed is false in the source evidence. If the manifest is present and has a truthy pass value but lacks state, select_policy() computes exposure=false and connected=false, then returns v434-android-wifi-policy-enable-gate-reconsider-pass. This creates a false-positive safety-gate result from incomplete, stale, dry-run, or attacker-planted evidence. In the project context, this does not directly execute code, but it can undermine the Wi-Fi containment gate and allow later work to proceed under an unsafe assumption.

title: Incomplete Wi-Fi evidence redaction leaks SSID fragments
description: The commit adds scripts/revalidation/wifi_android_control_gate_v432.py, which captures `cmd wifi status` and filtered `dumpsys wifi` output. Those outputs commonly contain the currently connected SSID or saved-network identifiers. The code attempts to redact SSID/BSSID/password-like fields, but the generic field regex uses `([^\s\]]+)`, so it stops at the first space. For example, `SSID: "My Home Wifi"` becomes `SSID=<redacted> Home Wifi"`, leaking most of the SSID. The same redacted-but-still-leaking text is persisted to command evidence files and included in the manifest/summary paths. Although EvidenceStore writes private files, this repository workflow explicitly produces evidence artifacts that may be reviewed or shared, so incomplete redaction can disclose sensitive network/location metadata.

title: Android property captures can leak unredacted Wi-Fi secrets
description: The V431 runtime gap collector adds a broad `getprop` capture filtered for `persist.`, `vendor.`, `wifi`, `wlan`, and related terms. Android `getprop` normally emits entries as `[property.key]: [value]`. The redaction regex only redacts sensitive tokens when the token is immediately followed by ':' or '=', e.g. `ssid=value` or `password: value`. It does not redact bracketed Android property output such as `[persist.vendor.wifi.password]: [secret]` or `[wifi.ssid]: [SecretSSID]` because the sensitive token is followed by `]` before the colon. The captured text is then written directly into private evidence files and also included in capture metadata via the same redaction routine. This violates the collector's stated guardrail that SSID/passphrase-like fields are redacted and can disclose Wi-Fi identifiers or password-like values if such properties exist on the device or are injected by a device under test.

title: Handoff failures can skip native boot rollback
description: The V430 wrapper promises to temporarily boot Android and then restore the native init boot image. It does attempt emergency rollback for two early post-write failures: a failed Android flash command and a failed readback hash check. However, after the Android boot image has been written and verified, the loop falls through to a generic `if execute and not step.ok: return ...` path. Failures in later steps such as `reboot-android`, `wait-android`, `wait-android-before-rollback`, `reboot-recovery-for-rollback`, or `wait-rollback-recovery` therefore return immediately without invoking the `restore-native` step. A malicious or simply broken Android boot image/device state that prevents ADB from returning can leave the device boot partition containing the Android image despite the rollback contract, impacting device integrity and availability of the native-init recovery workflow.

title: Rollback skipped when settle step fails after Android flash
description: The V425 runner flashes an Android boot image, boots Android, waits for sys.boot_completed=1, then performs a new settle-after-boot-complete step before running the V423 inventory and finally restoring native init. The script has explicit rollback-continuation handling for wait-boot-complete failures and V423 inventory failures, but not for the newly added settle step. If the settle getprop snapshot fails because ADB briefly disconnects, times out, or returns nonzero after Android has already booted, the generic failure path returns immediately. At that point the later wait-android-before-rollback, reboot-recovery-for-rollback, wait-rollback-recovery, and restore-native steps have not run, so the device remains on the temporary Android boot image despite the tool's bounded handoff/rollback safety expectation.

title: V416 gate ignores unsafe upstream execution flags
description: wifi_v416_current_gate_packet.py preserves upstream safety flags in each input summary, but its only boundary check blocks on device_mutations or wifi_bringup_executed. It does not block or review evidence where device_commands_executed, daemon_start_executed, or wifi_hal_start_executed are true. The final manifest then unconditionally reports those top-level flags as false. A manipulated or unsafe upstream evidence manifest can therefore be laundered into a passing V416 gate decision and required approval phrase, despite the workflow relying on these gates to prevent unauthorized daemon/HAL starts or device-side actions. Exploitation requires the attacker to influence local evidence files and the operator or automation to trust the generated gate packet, so this is not direct remote code execution, but it is an integrity failure in a privileged device-operation approval workflow.

title: Untrusted evidence path can hang comparator or read host files
description: The added host-side comparator constructs the live-output path directly from the V411 manifest directory and the manifest-controlled live_result.file field. Because absolute paths, ../ traversal, symlinks, FIFOs, device nodes, and very large files are not rejected, a crafted V411 manifest can make the tool read outside the evidence directory. The read is performed with Path.read_text() before the script validates the V411 decision or V414 target state, so even evidence that will later be classified as blocked can still trigger the read. In practice this can hang or exhaust memory by pointing at files such as /dev/zero, /dev/random, a FIFO, or a very large file. It can also cause limited host information disclosure if extracted fqinstance-looking strings from an arbitrary host file are written into the private V415 manifest/summary and later collected as evidence artifacts.

title: Unbounded VINTF version range expansion can exhaust memory
description: The commit adds a host-side parser for V413 VINTF XML captures. It treats the captured VINTF XML as input and, for version strings matching N.M-K, constructs every minor version with range(start_minor, end_minor + 1). There is no upper bound on the end value or on the total number of generated runtime_match_patterns. Because V413 captures are ultimately derived from device-side files/evidence and the script also accepts a manifest path, a malicious or tampered evidence set can include a Wi-Fi HAL with a version such as 2.0-1000000000. When V414 parses that record, it attempts to materialize a billion strings, causing excessive CPU and memory use and likely terminating the process or destabilizing the host. The fix should reject unreasonable version ranges, enforce a small maximum range width consistent with VINTF semantics, and fail closed on malformed or oversized numeric components before list construction.

title: Broken Wi-Fi surface regexes bypass readiness blockers
description: The collector intends to verify that no Wi-Fi-like network devices and no servicemanager/hwservicemanager/Wi-Fi daemon processes are active before reporting the VINTF declaration inventory as ready. However, both regexes use raw strings with double backslashes, e.g. `r"\\b(...)\\b"` and `r"wlan\\S*"`. In Python regex syntax this matches literal backslash sequences rather than word boundaries, whitespace, non-whitespace, or digits. As a result, normal lines such as `wlan0:` or `hwservicemanager` do not match. `active_surface()` therefore returns empty lists, `manager-wifi-process-surface-clean` passes instead of blocking, and `decide()` can return `v413-vintf-wifi-declarations-ready` on a dirty live device. This is a validation/safety-gate bypass rather than direct code execution, but it undermines the repository's stated no-Wi-Fi-bring-up and clean-surface controls.

title: V412 router trusts manifest-controlled live output paths
description: The newly added host-side router accepts a V411 manifest path and then constructs the live output path directly from the manifest's `live_result.file`. The value is not required to be relative, is not normalized back under the manifest/evidence directory, is not checked to be a regular file, and is read in full with `Path.read_text()`. Because `Path(parent) / absolute_path` resolves to the absolute path, a crafted manifest can point the router at files such as `/etc/...`, `/dev/zero`, a FIFO, or a very large file. This can disclose Wi-Fi-looking tokens and file-existence/path information into the generated evidence, and more importantly can hang or exhaust memory when reading special or large files. The router then bases a successful `wifi-service-candidates-ready` decision on parsed content from that attacker-selected file. The fix should constrain `live_result.file` to a relative path inside the V411 evidence directory, reject `..` escapes and absolute paths, require a regular file, and impose a conservative size limit before reading.

title: Stale evidence manifests can bypass V411 safety gates
description: The executor accepts an existing --out-dir, only chmods it, and does not remove or isolate previous step evidence. After running the deploy and query-preflight subprocesses, it ignores the StepResult rc/ok values and calls deploy_ok() or query_preflight_ok(), which simply load manifest.json from the step directory and check decision/pass. If a prior successful run, or a local attacker with write access to the evidence directory, leaves a passing deploy/manifest.json or query-preflight/manifest.json behind, and the current subprocess crashes, times out, or otherwise fails before writing a fresh manifest, the wrapper can treat the stale manifest as current success and proceed. In full/live mode this can advance to the live query step, which starts service-manager and Wi-Fi HAL components on the rooted device, bypassing the intended fail-closed preflight/deploy gate. The wrapper should either require a fresh empty run directory, delete per-step output before executing, require the subprocess rc to be zero, and/or validate that the loaded manifest was generated by the current step invocation.

title: V408 gate accepts spoofed no-bringup markers
description: The V408 runner is intended to classify whether prior V407 evidence preserved the no-bringup boundary before routing later Wi-Fi gates. However, it checks the boundary by calling has_all(text, boundary_needles), which only verifies that strings such as wifi_hal_composite_start.scan_connect_linkup=0 appear somewhere in the transcript. It does not parse these fields as authoritative key/value records, does not reject duplicate or later contradictory values, and does not require the final parsed value to be 0. As a result, a transcript containing both wifi_hal_composite_start.scan_connect_linkup=0 and wifi_hal_composite_start.scan_connect_linkup=1 is still accepted for the no-bringup-boundary check. If an attacker or compromised evidence source can influence the transcript consumed by this host-side gate, they can cause an unsafe or inconsistent V407 run to be classified as v408-hal-registration-service-surface-evidence-ready, weakening the workflow safety boundary.

title: V404 packet executes helper before validating its hash
description: The V404 readiness packet is documented as non-mutating and includes a `helper-v22` integrity check, but `run_read_only()` executes every command in `READ_ONLY_COMMANDS` before `live_checks()` evaluates the helper hash. That command list includes both `sha-helper` and then `helper-usage`, where `helper-usage` invokes `run <helper>` on the device. Because these commands are sent through the native root shell, a previously replaced helper at `/cache/bin/a90_android_execns_probe` would execute with root privileges before the script decides whether the hash matches. This turns the packet into an activation point for a persistent malicious helper in `/cache/bin`, contrary to the fail-closed/hash-gated intent.

title: Readiness packet ignores extra preflight blockers
description: The added `wifi_v392_current_readiness_packet.py` is intended to confirm that V392 is blocked only by the expected remote-helper/approval-gate conditions before recommending an exact-approved executor command. However, its deploy and live checks only verify the top-level decision string, one expected helper check, the approval-gate check, and the no-mutation flags. It does not inspect the rest of the input manifest checks for other non-passing `blocker` entries such as native health failures, active service-manager processes, Wi-Fi link surface, missing runtime materials, property-root failures, or binder-node contamination. As a result, a manifest with `decision=execns-helper-v21-deploy-blocked`, `remote-helper-v21=needs-deploy`, and `approval-gate=needs-operator` still passes readiness even if it also contains unrelated blocked safety checks. The same issue exists for the live preflight path. The packet then returns `v392-current-readiness-ready-for-approval` and emits an `--apply --assume-yes` recommended command. The downstream executor appears to rerun preflights, which limits direct exploitability, but this commit introduces a false-positive safety/approval gate that can mislead operators or automation into granting approval in an unsafe state.

title: V392 router trusts failed manifests for HAL readiness
description: The router extracts the executor manifest pass field, but the HAL-readiness branch does not require it to be true. It also accepts any decision string containing service-manager-start-only-live-pass or ending in hal-readiness-next-ready. As a result, a forged, stale, or inconsistent executor manifest such as {"decision":"attacker-hal-readiness-next-ready","pass":false,"wifi_bringup_executed":false} is routed as v392-post-live-router-hal-start-only-packet-ready with pass=true and the reason "service-manager start-only path is clean". The tool is host-only and does not directly execute device commands, so this is not immediate code execution. However, it undermines the evidence-based safety gate that is supposed to keep Wi-Fi HAL/start work blocked until clean V392 evidence exists. In workflows or automation that consume this router's manifest, this can cause unsafe progression to the next privileged device-operation approval step.

title: Framechain ELF resolver allows path traversal outside roots
description: The commit adds automatic ELF root discovery and path suffix rewriting for framechain map rows. A run log controls capture.crash.maprow.frameN_ra.path. If that path contains a helper namespace segment such as '/root/../../../../bin/ls', path_suffixes() extracts the suffix after '/root/' and root_candidates() joins it directly to an auto-discovered root. The code then accepts candidate.is_file() and invokes addr2line on that path. Because the candidate is never resolved and checked for containment under the intended ELF root, '..' components can escape the cache root and make the analyzer process arbitrary host files as ELF inputs. In the default V393 flow this is more exposed because auto ELF roots are enabled unless --no-auto-elf-cache is supplied. Practical impact is bounded: the attacker needs the operator to analyze a malicious or attacker-influenced run log, and addr2line is run with an argument list and a timeout. Still, this creates a host-side file probing / parser-exposure primitive and can leak symbol/source metadata for host ELF files or trigger addr2line parser bugs/DoS on unintended files.

title: Crash symbolizer allows ELF root path traversal
description: scripts/revalidation/wifi_service_manager_crash_symbolize.py parses capture.crash.maprow.*.path values from the supplied run log and passes the resolved file to addr2line. For absolute map paths it strips only the leading slash and appends the remainder to each --elf-root; for relative paths it appends the raw path directly. Because the result is never resolved and checked to remain under the ELF root, a crafted log can use paths such as /../../../../bin/sh or ../../../../some/file to escape the chosen root. If the escaped path exists, the tool invokes addr2line on that host file. This crosses the intended artifact boundary, can leak symbol/source metadata from unintended host binaries, can corrupt analysis results, and unnecessarily exposes host-side binary parsers to attacker-selected files.

title: Handoff docs re-enable unsafe NCM auto-interface setup
description: The newly added handoff document recommends `python3 scripts/revalidation/ncm_host_setup.py setup --allow-auto-interface` for optional NCM setup. That flag is explicitly the opt-in escape hatch for automatic host interface selection. In the associated script, the target host interface is selected from `ncm.host_addr` parsed from device/helper output, and the selected interface is then passed to sudo-capable `ip addr replace ... dev <interface>` and `ip link set <interface> up`. A malicious or compromised device helper, or spoofed bridge output, can report the MAC address of an unintended host NIC, causing the operator's host to reconfigure the wrong interface when following the new documentation. The safer workflow is to require `--interface <known USB/NCM ifname>` or, at minimum, document `--allow-auto-interface` only as a trusted single-device diagnostic fallback with an explicit warning.

title: Stale manifests can bypass V384 preflight gating
description: The executor runs child tools, records their return code in StepResult, but then decides whether to continue by re-reading fixed manifest paths under the selected output directory. It does not require the just-executed step to have rc == 0, does not clear old manifests before running, and does not bind manifests to the current invocation. If the output directory contains stale pass manifests and the current deploy or live-preflight subprocess fails before writing a fresh manifest, deploy_ok() or live_preflight_ok() can still return true. In an approved `full` run this can advance to the live service-manager ptrace-lite command despite the current deploy/preflight having timed out or failed. This does not bypass the exact approval phrase requirement, but it does bypass the safety checks that are supposed to fail closed before root device commands/daemon-start attempts.

title: Ptrace capture can run with stale V373 approval
description: The commit adds a V384 wrapper with a new exact approval phrase for ptrace-lite service-manager crash capture, and the report states older V382/V373 phrases are not sufficient. However, the underlying reusable runner was also extended to accept `--capture-mode ptrace-lite` while its `approved()` function still compares against the imported V373 approval phrase. If helper v15 is already deployed, or the caller overrides `--helper-sha256` to match it, invoking the generic runner with the old V373 phrase, `--capture-mode ptrace-lite`, `--apply`, and `--assume-yes` causes `approved()` to return true. The runner then appends both `--capture-mode ptrace-lite` and `--allow-service-manager-start-only`, satisfying the C helper's only ptrace-lite authorization check. This bypasses the intended V384-specific approval wrapper and permits bounded service-manager execution under ptrace, including collection of registers, maps, status, and crash metadata, using a stale approval phrase.

title: Stale manifests can bypass V382 executor safety gates
description: The executor runs child scripts and then decides whether to proceed by reading fixed manifest paths under --out-dir. It does not delete old manifests before running a step, does not require the just-run StepResult to be successful, and does not validate freshness/current run metadata in the manifest. If a previous successful manifest remains, or a local attacker who can write the evidence directory plants one, a child step can fail or time out while the executor still observes the stale pass decision. The most security-relevant case is live-preflight: with an approved live invocation, a stale live-preflight/manifest.json containing service-manager-start-only-live-preflight-ready/pass=true causes the executor to run the live command even when the current live preflight failed or was not actually performed. The same pattern affects final readiness and deploy checks in full/deploy flows. This weakens the fail-closed guard intended to ensure the device is in a clean, bounded state before root/device-mutating service-manager smoke actions are started.

title: V382 readiness gate executes device commands while reporting none
description: The added readiness gate runs the V382 deploy preflight as part of a supposedly host-only approval check. That preflight is not host-only: it calls run_read_only_preflight(), which sends cmdv1 commands to the native-init device shell, including running /cache/bin/toybox and /cache/bin/a90_android_execns_probe. The new aggregator explicitly disables the no-device-execution requirement for this step, treats an absent device_commands_executed field as acceptable, and then writes its own top-level manifest with device_commands_executed set to false unconditionally. This can mislead operators or automation into believing no device-side root commands were executed before approval. If persistent helpers such as /cache/bin/toybox or /cache/bin/a90_android_execns_probe have been tampered with, running this allegedly host-only gate may execute attacker-controlled code as root on the device. Even without helper tampering, the audit/approval boundary is bypassed because root device commands occur during a no-approval readiness command.

title: Serial fallback bypasses helper install path validation
description: This commit adds an automatic serial fallback for helper deployment. The previous NCM path delegates installation to tcpctl_host.py, whose command_install() calls validate_install_target() to restrict installs to /cache/bin/, /cache/a90-runtime/bin/, or /mnt/sdext/a90/bin/ and reject unsafe paths. The new run_serial_install() path directly derives target, target_dir, staging, and tmp_target from args.remote_helper and then removes, decodes, chmods, mv -f's, hashes, and executes that target without equivalent validation. Because transfer_method defaults to auto, any NCM reachability failure silently selects this unvalidated serial path. A caller who can influence --remote-helper, --local-helper, and --helper-sha256 for an approved run can cause root-side writes/moves outside the documented /cache/bin helper replacement boundary. This is constrained by the lab workflow and existing root control channel, but it weakens the script's guardrail that the approval phrase authorizes only replacing /cache/bin/a90_android_execns_probe.

title: V373 preflight can run a mutable helper before approval
description: The added `wifi_service_manager_start_only_smoke.py` advertises preflight as read-only and fail-closed, but `PREFLIGHT_COMMANDS` includes `run /cache/bin/a90_android_execns_probe` without arguments. That path is reached by the `preflight` subcommand with no approval phrase and with no hash/version validation of the helper. Because `/cache/bin` is persistent mutable device storage in this project, a stale or attacker-replaced helper would execute as root during what operators expect to be a non-mutating safety check. The script then decides whether the required mode exists by searching the helper's output for a substring, so a malicious helper can both execute code and spoof the capability check. Separately, if blocker checks are present, `decide()` still returns `pass=True`, and `main()` exits 0, so shell/CI automation using the process status as the safety gate can continue despite `service-manager-start-only-smoke-blocked`.

title: Approval packet treats failed safety probes as clean
description: The added approval-packet script is intended to be a fail-closed gate before a later service-manager start-only smoke. However, several safety checks do not require the underlying captures to succeed. If the `ps` capture fails or returns no text, `manager_process_lines()` returns an empty list and `service-manager-processes-clean` passes. If `/proc/net/dev` cannot be captured, `wifi-link-surface-clean` also passes. The Binder-node check treats any failed `stat` capture as absence, without distinguishing an expected ENOENT result from a transport/protocol/tool failure. As a result, a broken or intentionally interfered-with probe can produce `process_count=0`, `wifi_link_count=0`, and `binder=False` and allow the manifest decision to become `service-manager-start-only-approval-packet-ready`. This weakens the approval boundary around starting privileged Android service-manager components and can lead automation/operators to proceed while the device state was not actually verified.

title: Live executor trusts stale smoke manifests after failed run
description: In `run` mode, the executor launches the V366 live smoke step, but it never checks the returned `StepResult` before routing the result. Instead, it immediately invokes the router against `args.live_out_dir / "manifest.json"` and decides success solely from the router decision. Because `--live-out-dir` is user-controlled and defaults to a stable directory, a stale manifest from a previous successful run, or a forged/unwritable manifest left in that directory, can cause the router to return `runtime-repair-smoke-router-service-runtime-next-ready` even if the current live smoke subprocess failed, timed out, crashed, or could not write fresh evidence. This breaks the intended fail-closed behavior of the executor and can falsely advance the workflow toward the next privileged service-manager approval step.

title: Cleanup failure ignored by smoke result router
description: The newly added wifi_runtime_repair_smoke_result_router.py accepts and loads --cleanup-manifest, but route_result() makes its safety decision solely from the approval packet and smoke manifest. If a stale or previously successful smoke manifest is present while the cleanup manifest indicates a later cleanup failure or dirty postflight state, the router still returns runtime-repair-smoke-router-service-runtime-next-ready with pass=true. This can incorrectly advance the workflow toward a later service-manager start-only approval packet even though cleanup evidence says temporary nodes or other postflight state may still be unsafe. The router itself does not mutate the device, so exploitation requires an operator or automation to trust its output, but this is a safety-gate bypass in the privileged device-control workflow.

title: Guarded smoke runner can delete live device nodes
description: The added `wifi_runtime_repair_smoke.py` is intended to be fail-closed and only create temporary device nodes after preflight validation and an exact approval phrase. In practice, `build_manifest()` executes the mutation block as soon as the approval phrase and flags are present, before `build_checks()` evaluates the V365 manifest, required inputs, `/proc/partitions` metadata, existing Binder nodes, service-manager state, or Wi-Fi cleanliness. Separately, the `cleanup` subcommand is exposed without the approval phrase and immediately runs `rm -f` over all hard-coded target nodes. The script also does not track which nodes it created: `create_nodes()` skips creation when a node already exists, but `cleanup_nodes()` still removes every path in `CLEANUP_PATHS`. As a result, running the new supposedly guarded helper can delete pre-existing `/dev/binder`, `/dev/hwbinder`, `/dev/vndbinder`, or `/dev/block/sda29`, or can create/use hard-coded device nodes even when preflight checks would later mark the run blocked.

title: V365 packet trusts unverified /cache/bin helpers
description: The added live-mode collector uses `run /cache/bin/toybox ...` for read-only checks before verifying the integrity of that binary, and it only `stat`s `/cache/bin/a90_android_execns_probe` before generating a future V366 command that runs it as root. In this project, `/cache/bin` is a persistent trust boundary: if a compromised host, prior control-channel exposure, recovery session, or other actor with write access replaces those helpers, V365 can still report the packet as ready and can immediately execute a malicious `toybox` during live collection. The generated repair steps then encourage later execution of an unverified `a90_android_execns_probe`. Presence checks do not establish artifact identity. The script should require device-side helper manifest verification or explicit SHA-256/marker checks for `toybox` and `a90_android_execns_probe` before any `run /cache/bin/...` command, and should block the packet if helper integrity cannot be proven.

title: Handoff linter can approve arbitrary commands
description: The added host-only linter is intended to validate that generated V340 preflight/live/cleanup commands safely invoke scripts/revalidation/wifi_private_property_namespace_proof.py with the expected approval phrase and output directories. However, command_view() scans the entire parsed argv for any item ending in wifi_private_property_namespace_proof.py and check_command() accepts that as the script. It never verifies argv[0]/argv[1], the Python interpreter, or that the runner path is actually the executable script. A malicious manifest command such as `python3 -c 'malicious code' scripts/revalidation/wifi_private_property_namespace_proof.py --out-dir ... --approval-phrase ... --allow-device-mutation --assume-yes run` is therefore marked PASS even though executing it runs the `-c` payload instead of the approved runner. The parser also takes the first occurrence of options like --out-dir, while argparse consumers normally use the last value, so duplicate arguments can hide the actual runtime values from the linter. This undermines the linter as a security gate before operator or automation execution of generated commands.

title: Transcript writes follow symlinks outside evidence dir
description: The helper creates its EvidenceStore using private-file helpers for manifest.json and summary.md, but it handles the transcripts subdirectory separately. If an attacker can pre-create the selected output directory contents in a shared checkout, CI workspace, or operator-controlled --out-dir, they can make transcripts a symlink to another directory, or place symlinks for the fixed transcript filenames. transcript_dir.mkdir(..., exist_ok=True) does not reject an existing symlink to a directory, and transcript_path.write_text() follows symlinks and creates files using the process umask rather than the EvidenceStore's private/no-follow writer. When the operator runs the helper, subprocess output is written through the symlink, potentially leaking transcripts outside the private evidence tree or overwriting files writable by the operator with predictable subprocess output.

title: Handoff accepts evidence when freshness git diff fails
description: The new host-only handoff packet is intended to accept stale prerequisite evidence only when `git diff <evidence_head>..HEAD` shows no changes to that gate's critical paths. However, `changed_paths_since()` returns an empty list for missing or `unknown` evidence heads and returns a synthetic `git-diff-failed: ...` string when `git diff` fails. `check_input()` then only treats paths matching the critical-path allowlist as affected, so a failed diff does not block the gate. As a result, a manifest with expected decision/pass fields, `git_dirty=false`, and an invalid or `unknown` `host.git_head` can be accepted as `stale-unaffected` and the final packet can report `v317-handoff-awaiting-approval`. This weakens the safety gate before an operator copies the generated live command.

title: Pre-live audit fails open on unverifiable evidence
description: The introduced audit is intended to accept old gate evidence only when `git diff <evidence_head>..HEAD` proves that critical implementation paths were not changed. However, `changed_paths_since()` returns an empty change list when `evidence_head` is missing or `unknown`, and returns a synthetic `git-diff-failed: ...` string when git cannot resolve the head. `evaluate_gate()` then filters that list only for paths matching the gate's critical paths; the synthetic failure string does not match, so `affected` is empty and the gate is marked `stale-unaffected`. The final `ok` check never requires a present, valid, diffable `evidence_head`, so manifests with correct decision/pass/device flags but no valid git provenance can pass. An attacker or compromised local process that can write the repo's evidence directory can forge V325-V335 manifests and cause the pre-live gate to return `v317-prelive-gate-awaiting-approval`, potentially misleading an operator into proceeding with a privileged live device proof.

title: Host-only Wi-Fi regression can run device commands
description: The added wifi_approval_gate_regression.py script includes a V320 case named v320-run-full-approval-still-blocked-v317 that passes the exact V320 approval phrase together with --allow-device-mutation and --assume-yes to wifi_private_property_lookup_proof.py. The regression assumes V317 PASS evidence is missing, but it does not isolate or override --v317-manifest to a known-missing fixture. If the normal default tmp/wifi/v317-private-property-namespace-proof/manifest.json already records a passing V317 proof, and the V312 prerequisites are present, the child V320 proof reaches its live run path and calls run_cmdv1_command against the device bridge. This violates the script's documented host-only/no-device-command safety property. The hard refusal guard only blocks the V317 full-approval combination and does not block the V320 full-approval live combination.

title: Router can approve V320 from inconsistent V317 evidence
description: The router is intended to fail closed unless V317 has passed. However, decide() checks decision == "private-property-namespace-proof-pass" and bool(v317.get("pass")) before considering live_error. As a result, a malformed or attacker-modified V317 manifest with decision set to the pass string, pass set to a truthy value, and live_error set will still produce post-v317-router-v320-ready. The emitted V320 run command is not merely a plan; it includes the exact approval phrase, --allow-device-mutation, and --assume-yes. The downstream V320 runner treats those arguments as satisfying its approval gate and will execute the device helper if its other evidence gates also pass. This is not direct remote code execution by the router itself, but it is a security-relevant fail-open routing/authorization flaw in an evidence-driven root-device workflow.

title: Stale evidence can approve live readiness packets
description: The commit adds scripts/revalidation/wifi_v317_live_readiness_packet.py, which is intended to gate a mutation-enabled live command on fresh host-only evidence. However, the V330 freshness check only verifies top-level fields such as decision, pass, and device mutation flags. It does not verify that the V330 manifest was generated for the current git HEAD or that it was generated from a clean tree. The script later collects current host metadata, but only stores it in the output manifest after the readiness decision has already been made. As a result, an old or forged V330 manifest with decision=wifi-evidence-freshness-clean can cause the script to emit decision=v317-live-readiness-packet-ready even if the current worktree has changed since the prerequisite evidence was generated. The generated packet includes a command with --allow-device-mutation and --assume-yes, so this fail-open freshness check can undermine the intended operator safety gate if an operator follows the packet.

title: Freshness audit ignores dirty current checkout
description: The v330 audit is intended to prove that V325-V329 evidence is fresh for the current clean git head. However, the implementation only compares each prior manifest's recorded git_head to the current HEAD string and verifies that the prior manifest recorded git_dirty=false. It collects the current host metadata, including the current checkout's git_dirty value, but never uses that value to block the decision. As a result, if the old evidence manifests match HEAD, the audit can still emit wifi-evidence-freshness-clean even when the operator is running from a dirty working tree containing uncommitted or attacker-inserted changes. In this repository, these evidence reports are used as safety gates before privileged device operations, so a false clean result weakens the integrity of the validation chain.

title: Approval gate accepts truthy string booleans
description: The new host-side approval packet generator validates prerequisite manifests using Python truthiness instead of strict JSON booleans. In build_checks(), bool(v317.get("pass")) and bool(v323.get("audit_pass")) both evaluate to True for non-empty strings, including "false". Likewise, not bool(v323.get("chain_ready")) treats a missing/null chain_ready as equivalent to false. As a result, a local attacker or compromised evidence-generation step that can influence the referenced manifest files can cause this script to emit a successful private-property approval refresh packet and return exit code 0 even when the source evidence explicitly says pass/audit_pass are false. The script itself does not execute device commands, but it is part of a live-operation approval workflow; a false-ready packet can mislead automation or an operator into approving subsequent root device mutations. The checks should require exact types and values, e.g. v317.get("pass") is True, v323.get("audit_pass") is True, and v323.get("chain_ready") is False.

title: Unverified helper path executed as device root
description: This commit changes the proof runner from a fail-closed planning skeleton into a live execution path. When the prerequisite evidence and approval gates pass, it sends a root `run` command over the serial bridge. The executable path comes directly from `--helper-path`, defaulting to `/cache/bin/a90_android_execns_probe`, and is not constrained to the expected helper nor verified by hash, marker, helper manifest, or version output before execution. Because `/cache/bin` is persistent helper storage in this project, a replaced helper or attacker-chosen device path can be executed as root/PID1-context by this supposedly bounded property-lookup proof. The fixed arguments limit accidental misuse of normal binaries, but a malicious replacement binary can ignore those arguments and perform arbitrary actions. This weakens the documented safety boundary that the runner only performs a read-only allowlisted property lookup.

title: appendfile scope can be bypassed through path traversal races
description: The commit adds a root/PID1 appendfile primitive intended to be scoped to runtime/cache/temp workspaces. The allow check only verifies that the user-supplied string starts with one of the allowed prefixes and does not contain a literal /../ segment. The subsequent open() uses O_NOFOLLOW, but that only rejects a symlink as the final path component; it does not prevent following symlinks in parent directories. Because one allowed prefix is under /tmp, which is mounted mode=1777, and because writable runtime/workspace trees may be influenced by other processes or races, an attacker able to place or race an intermediate symlink can cause appendfile to append root-controlled data to files outside the intended allowed directories. This breaks the security property claimed by the new scoped primitive. A robust implementation should resolve/walk paths with openat/openat2 using no-symlink/beneath semantics, verify parent directories with fstat, avoid world-writable prefixes, and restrict writes to a freshly-created private transfer directory.

title: Safety audit accepts malformed manifest fields
description: The v317 safety audit is intended to be fail-closed before live privileged device mutations. However, decision_check() uses bool(manifest.get("pass")) instead of requiring the JSON field to be an actual boolean equal to the expected value. For refusal manifests, a missing pass field is treated as False and therefore passes. For plan manifests, any truthy non-boolean value such as the string "false" is accepted as True. mutation_check() similarly defaults missing commands to [] and treats a missing device_mutations field as false, so incomplete manifests can satisfy the no-mutation check. A crafted set of manifests can therefore produce private-property-namespace-proof-audit-pass without actually proving that the refusal manifests contained pass=false, device_mutations=false, or command records. This undermines the commit's safety-audit gate and can mislead an operator or automation relying on the audit result before approving privileged device work.

title: Truthy manifest pass values bypass v316 approval checks
description: The added approval-packet script loads prior v314/v315 manifests and gates the v317 approval packet on their decision strings plus bool(manifest.get("pass")). In Python, non-empty strings such as "false" or "0" are truthy, so the check accepts malformed JSON manifests that explicitly say pass is false if encoded as a string. Because the script also allows operator-specified manifest paths, a malicious or corrupted manifest can make the tool emit decision private-property-live-approval-ready and the exact v317 approval phrase even though the prior gates did not truly pass. This commit does not execute device commands by itself, but it weakens a safety/authorization workflow intended to prevent premature privileged device mutation in later live proof steps.

title: Spoofable v312 manifest can bypass approval readiness gate
description: The added script is intended to gate the next live mutation boundary by confirming that v312 produced a valid private property runtime layout. However, `load_json()` accepts any JSON from `--v312-manifest`, and `build_checks()` treats the prerequisite as satisfied solely when `decision == private-property-layout-dryrun-ready` and `len(files) >= 5`. It does not require `v312['pass'] == true`, does not verify that `files` is a list of file records, and does not verify that the referenced files exist or match expected hashes. Because strings and dictionaries also have a length, a malformed manifest such as `{ "decision": "private-property-layout-dryrun-ready", "pass": false, "files": "xxxxx" }` is accepted and causes the tool to exit successfully with `private-property-materialization-approval-ready`. While this commit does not itself perform device mutation, it introduces a bypassable safety approval artifact that may be consumed by the next materialization step and can mislead an operator or automation into treating an invalid or tampered layout as approved for a privileged device-side operation.

title: Manifest context allows host file overwrite
description: scripts/revalidation/wifi_private_property_layout_dryrun.py treats each successful v311 mapping context as a filename under layout/dev/__properties__. However, it writes the file in the loop over by_context before running the context-filenames safety check. A malicious or corrupted v311 manifest can set context to an absolute path such as /tmp/pwned or a traversal path containing ../. Path joining does not enforce containment, and write_private_bytes opens the destination with O_TRUNC, so the script can create or truncate arbitrary files writable by the user running the dry-run. The script may later report private-property-layout-dryrun-blocked, but the out-of-tree write has already happened.

title: Rescue doctor ignores selected ADB serial
description: The added android_capture_rescue_doctor.py accepts --serial, but adb_probe() always runs plain `adb devices -l` and returns every attached ADB target. decide() then makes decisions from the aggregate states, and a successful native bridge probe takes priority over any ADB ambiguity. build_commands() also emits the live-handoff command directly from the v304 manifest/fallback without adding the selected serial. In a shared lab or malicious-USB scenario, an unrelated recovery/Android ADB device can affect the doctor's decision or be left unscoped in the recommended live handoff. If the operator follows that recommendation, the existing handoff executor treats any recovery-state ADB line as satisfying the wait and then performs unscoped `adb push`/`adb shell dd ... of=/dev/block/by-name/boot`, which can flash the wrong attached recovery device or leave the intended target in an unsafe state. The doctor itself is read-only, so exploitation still requires operator execution of the generated command, but the new classifier/recommendation path undermines the explicit serial-selection safety boundary.

title: Live guard endorses untrusted command from manifest
description: scripts/revalidation/android_capture_live_guard.py treats the local v302 manifest as the source of truth for the destructive live handoff command. The only validation applied to live_command is that it is non-empty and contains the substrings --allow-android-boot-flash, --assume-yes, and --i-understand-native-rollback. If a stale or tampered v302 manifest is supplied or modified in the workspace, the guard can return GO and write an attacker-chosen command to live-command.txt, provided the command includes those substrings. Because the documented workflow asks an operator to execute the generated live command after approval, this can turn artifact tampering into destructive device operations or arbitrary host-side command execution by operator copy/paste. The guard should compare against an exact expected command or parse and allowlist argv, including script path, subcommand, output directory, and permitted flags, rather than copying manifest-controlled text.

title: Out-dir handling can chmod arbitrary parent directories
description: scripts/revalidation/android_capture_handoff_preflight.py introduces a new CLI entry point with a user-controlled --out-dir. In main(), that path is converted with repo_path() and passed directly to EvidenceStore. repo_path() preserves absolute paths, so an operator or attacker-influenced command can target locations outside the repository. EvidenceStore.__init__ then calls ensure_private_dir(run_dir.parent), and ensure_private_dir unconditionally chmods the path it is given to 0700. As a result, running the new script with --out-dir /tmp/some-run changes /tmp itself to 0700. If run with elevated privileges or pointed at shared/system directories, this can deny other users or services access to critical directories and can also unexpectedly alter permissions on arbitrary existing directories.

title: Raw property cat allows cmdv1 marker injection
description: The newly added wifi_property_snapshot_model.py discovers paths under /mnt/system and then executes `toybox cat` on each discovered property/context file through the cmdv1/A90P1 serial protocol. The cmdv1 host parser treats any `A90P1 END ...` marker followed later by a prompt-looking `a90:/#` as command completion. Because raw file contents are streamed through the same channel, a malicious or attacker-controlled mounted system image can place those marker strings inside a build.prop/property_contexts file. When the tool cats that file, the host can prematurely parse attacker-controlled END fields as a successful command result, truncate the evidence, and make automation decisions from spoofed output. If the discovered path is a symlink or huge/special file, the same unvalidated cat workflow can also cause long-running or unbounded output, leaving PID1 waiting on the child after the host-side timeout. This is most relevant when the operator runs the collector against an untrusted or compromised device/system image, which is within the repository threat model for manipulated device output consumed by host tooling.

title: Prerequisite gate passes with missing Binder smoke input
description: The added script is intended to require the prior v292 Binder open-only smoke result before service-manager planning. However, `load_manifest()` silently converts a missing manifest into `{present: false}`, `build_checks()` marks the missing v292 decision as a blocker, and `choose_decision()` then ignores blockers in `plan` mode and returns success. In `run` mode, once any native capture succeeds, the same missing v292 blocker is folded into `service-manager-prereq-blockers-mapped` with `pass=True`. Because `main()` exits 0 whenever `pass_ok` is true, CI or follow-on operator tooling that gates on this result can advance even when the Binder prerequisite was never proven. This is a safety/security gate bypass in a workflow that controls a rooted device and prepares future service-manager execution.

title: Binder smoke runs destructive cleanup before validation
description: scripts/revalidation/wifi_binder_open_smoke.py loads candidate major/minor values from a v290 manifest, then in run --apply mode immediately sends root commands to create /dev/binder, /dev/hwbinder, and /dev/vndbinder, opens them, and unconditionally removes those paths in a finally block. The prerequisite decision check for the v291 manifest is only performed afterward by choose_decision(). load_candidates() also accepts candidates solely by name/path/type and does not require the v290 manifest decision or each candidate's status to be ready. As a result, a stale or attacker-supplied v290 manifest can cause the tool to remove pre-existing Binder device nodes even when the v291 prerequisite is missing or invalid. Because the native mknodc implementation treats EEXIST as success without verifying ownership/type/device number, existing Binder nodes can be considered successfully 'created' and then deleted by the host tool's cleanup step. In this lab workflow the impact is constrained, but it can degrade device availability/integrity and violates the intended guardrail that prerequisite evidence be accepted before any privileged device mutation.

title: Binder smoke can delete pre-existing devnodes
description: The script is intended to temporarily create Binder character devices and clean up only what it created. However, in run --apply mode it captures pre-state, immediately attempts mknodc, and always removes all candidate paths in a finally block. The pass/fail logic never checks that the pre-state stat commands failed/confirmed absence.
