This is dangerous because the native mknodc helper treats an existing node as success, so if the target device already has Binder devnodes, or if the default/stale v290 manifest no longer matches the current device state, the smoke can remove legitimate Binder nodes. That can cause Binder IPC/service availability failures on the device and corrupt the evidence by reporting binder-devnode-create-cleanup-pass after deleting nodes it did not create. The script should fail closed if any pre-state Binder node exists, validate live major/minor metadata before applying, and track exactly which nodes were newly created before deleting them.

title: V284 observer skips CNSS helper integrity preflight
description: The new v284 tool directly starts the CNSS start-only helper through the serial root shell once the tcpctl side-channel is ready. Although it exposes --helper-sha256 and passes helper_sha256 into a temporary Namespace, that value is not used by helper_start_argv(), and the script never delegates to the existing wifi_cnss_start_only_runner.build_manifest() preflight that verifies /cache/bin/a90_android_execns_probe with sha256sum and blocks live execution on mismatch. As a result, a stale, attacker-replaced, or otherwise untrusted helper at /cache/bin/a90_android_execns_probe will be executed as root when an operator runs the v284 observer with the required live-run approval flags. This weakens the intended artifact-integrity guard around a dangerous root/device bring-up workflow.

title: Read-only guardrail regex can be bypassed with --toybox
description: The new script is documented and reported as a no-start/read-only ICNSS/WLFW observer, and it attempts to enforce that with DENIED_COMMAND_PATTERNS. However, several denied patterns are written as `\b/...`, for example `\b/vendor/bin/cnss-daemon\b` and `\b/sys/bus/platform/drivers/icnss/...`. In Python regex syntax, a word boundary does not match between whitespace/start-of-string and '/', because both sides are non-word positions. As a result, absolute denied paths are not detected. Since the script exposes `--toybox` and substitutes that value into every `run` command before validation and execution, a caller can set `--toybox /vendor/bin/cnss-daemon` or another side-effecting absolute executable and bypass the intended no-daemon/no-control guardrail. This does not create a new unauthenticated remote command channel by itself, but it undermines the safety boundary of a tool whose purpose is to be read-only and no-start.

title: CNSS start runs before QCA preflight failures are enforced
description: In run mode, the observer first records the before QCA/WLAN snapshot, but then immediately calls wifi_cnss_start_only_runner.build_manifest(..., "run") before evaluating the snapshot's critical checks. The code does define critical before-snapshot failures and classify() will return a failure for them, but that classification happens only after the delegated runner has already had a chance to execute the start-only helper. The delegated runner can run the helper when its own narrower gates pass. Those gates do not enforce all of the QCA observer's critical conditions, such as the expected native-init version check from the QCA snapshot, v277 prerequisite state, QCA compatibility visibility, WLAN parameter readability, or wiphy/rfkill readiness. As a result, a device in an unexpected or already unsafe state can still have cnss-daemon started during an approved v279 run, and the tool reports the blocker only after the live action has occurred. This violates the fail-closed safety model for a privileged device-root hardware/radio service start.

title: Classifier parses truncated command output
description: The new classifier records each command by calling capture_to_manifest(), which truncates the command text to the first 4096 bytes. The script then builds its analysis from those manifest records rather than from the original full capture text. As a result, safety checks that depend on potentially large outputs such as `ps -A -o pid,stat,comm` and `/proc/modules` can miss relevant lines if `cnss-daemon`, `cnss_diag`, or the `wlan` module appears after the truncation point. That can incorrectly mark the CNSS process table as clean or the WLAN module as unloaded, producing a false PASS for a guardrail-oriented classifier. The full output is written to the capture files, but it is not what the classification logic consumes.

title: QRTR readback trusts spoofable service events
description: The commit introduces bounded QRTR readback support, but the helper's readback parser does not authenticate or constrain the notifications it counts. readback_notifications() receives datagrams from the QRTR socket, decodes cmd, and increments service_events for any QRTR_TYPE_NEW_SERVER whose server tuple is non-zero. It does not check that the packet came from the QRTR control/nameservice port, and it does not compare the returned service/instance against the operator-approved lookup target. The runner then treats any service_events > 0 as a successful qrtr-ns-readback-services decision. A malicious or compromised QRTR peer that can send to the helper's port during the readback window could spoof or flood a NEW_SERVER-looking datagram and make the automation record that a service was observed. In this repository's workflow, that undermines the evidence/safety classifier intended to precede later QMI-control planning, potentially causing follow-up operations against a wrong or attacker-influenced endpoint. The direct impact is limited by explicit approval gates and short readback windows, but the integrity of the control-plane evidence is security-relevant.

title: Helper transfer HTTP server binds to all interfaces
description: The commit adds a short-lived helper deployment path that serves the locally built a90_qrtr_ns_probe binary over Python's ThreadingHTTPServer. The server has no authentication or client allowlist, serves the helper to any requester for the expected filename, and the default bind address is 0.0.0.0 even though the intended device URL host is the USB/NCM host address 192.168.7.1. In a shared lab or bridged network, a network-adjacent attacker can download the helper artifact and can also open many concurrent requests/connections against the unauthenticated threaded server during an approved run, causing transfer disruption or evidence ambiguity. This does not by itself provide root command execution, because the device-side SHA-256 check prevents a simple network MITM from replacing the helper, but it unnecessarily exposes a host-side service beyond the USB device boundary.

title: Weak approval contract validation can falsely pass gates
description: The v265 approval contract is intended to be a safety gate before any future QRTR nameservice packet transmission. However, its validation is not strict. The v264 prerequisite check uses bool(v264.get("pass")), so a manifest containing a string such as "false" is treated as passing. Separately, wildcard detection only compares service and instance against the exact strings "0" and "0". Numeric equivalents such as "0x0", "+0", or other forms a future runner might parse as zero are classified as non-wildcard. As a result, a crafted manifest or crafted service/instance arguments can cause the tool to emit decision `qrtr-nameservice-approval-contract-ready` and generate a future command containing the QRTR transmission approval flags, even though the approval contract should have blocked. This does not itself transmit packets, and the future runner is not present in this commit, but it weakens an explicit safety/authorization boundary that later automation or operators may trust.

title: QRTR probe executes helper before verifying its hash
description: The new script is intended to be a read-only/no-scan evidence collector and its plan says `/cache/bin/a90_qrtr_probe` is trusted only if its sha256 matches the known v250 value. However, the command list includes both `sha-helper` and `qrtr-probe`, and `capture_commands()` runs every command in that list without checking the sha result first. The hash comparison is only performed later while building manifest checks, after `/cache/bin/a90_qrtr_probe` has already been executed as root on the device. If an attacker can replace `/cache/bin/a90_qrtr_probe` or make the configured helper path point to a malicious binary, running this supposedly safe collector becomes a trigger for attacker-controlled root code execution and possible QRTR/Wi-Fi side effects before the script can fail the integrity check.

title: Approval packet skips live helper integrity check
description: The newly added wifi_cnss_live_approval_packet.py is intended to be a no-start safety gate before a future approved CNSS daemon launch. However, unlike the existing start-only runner preflight, it does not capture or validate the SHA-256 of /cache/bin/a90_android_execns_probe. The script accepts --helper-sha256 and records an expected value in the dry-run plan, but its READ_ONLY_COMMANDS omit the helper stat/sha256sum checks and its final gate list contains no helper integrity check. It then executes the helper via the root device shell in no-allow mode and trusts the helper's own printed cnss_start fields to decide that execution was blocked. If the persistent helper in /cache/bin is replaced after earlier prerequisite manifests were generated, a tampered helper can run during this supposedly no-start approval packet, spoof the expected no-allow output, and cause the tool to generate a live approval command that will later execute the same untrusted helper with --allow-cnss-start-only. This breaks the approval packet's trust boundary because stale prerequisite manifests are treated as sufficient evidence for a current executable.

title: CNSS property scanner misses mangled SetProperty writes
description: The v251 tool is used to classify cnss-daemon as having either a read-only property surface or a write/control property surface before considering live daemon startup. The plan explicitly lists android::base::SetProperty as a property write/control symbol to detect. However, readelf -Ws emits C++ symbols in mangled form by default, for example android::base::SetProperty appears as a name containing a length-prefixed fragment such as '11SetProperty'. The regex requires a word boundary before 'SetProperty', and the preceding '1' is a word character, so this does not match. As a result, a binary importing android::base::SetProperty can be misclassified as 'cnss-property-read-only-surface' because classify() only checks whether write_symbols is non-empty. This undermines the safety gate and can falsely report that no property write/control surface exists.

title: Preflight executes unverified helper before hash gate
description: The v248 preflight is documented as a guarded no-start validation step, but it collects the helper SHA and then unconditionally executes the helper via the device root `run` command before `build_primitive_checks()` evaluates whether the expected hash matched. If `/cache/bin/a90_android_execns_probe` has been replaced, the malicious helper is executed as root before the script reports the mismatch. Additionally, the SHA capture command is hardcoded to `/cache/bin/a90_android_execns_probe`, while the executable actually run comes from `args.helper`; a caller that supplies a different helper path can execute an unverified binary while the manifest still checks the hash of the default path. This weakens the intended fail-closed approval workflow and can turn helper tampering or unsafe wrapper-controlled arguments into root code execution during a supposedly no-start preflight.

title: Root helper can unlink attacker-chosen directories
description: The commit adds synthetic APEX materialization for --vndk-apex-alias-mode v30-to-current. That mode creates symlinks under paths->apex and later calls cleanup_dir_entries(paths->apex), which opens the path normally and unlinks every safe-looking entry below it. The helper's temporary base remains predictable (/tmp/a90-v231-<pid>) and mkdir_p accepts pre-existing path components without checking ownership, type, or symlink status. If an unprivileged local process can write to /tmp and pre-create /tmp/a90-v231-<predicted-pid>/root/apex as a symlink to a sensitive writable directory such as /cache/bin, the root helper will follow that symlink during cleanup and unlink entries from the target directory. The issue is gated on running the new alias mode, but that is the workflow introduced by this commit. Cleanup should use a securely created mkdtemp-style directory, reject pre-existing/symlink components with lstat/openat(O_NOFOLLOW), fail on EEXIST when creating synthetic APEX links, and delete only links that the helper actually created.

title: Untrusted manifest paths allow host file reads and DoS
description: The newly added `wifi_linker_namespace_gap_probe.py` loads user-supplied or evidence-bundle manifests and, for each matrix item, joins `manifest_path.parent` with the manifest's `output_file` field before calling `read_text()` on the result. In Python, an absolute `output_file` path discards the intended parent directory, and relative paths with `..` can escape it. The code also reads the whole target with no size limit, regular-file check, or timeout. A malicious manifest can therefore cause the host-side analysis tool to open arbitrary host paths or blocking/unbounded sources such as `/dev/zero`, a FIFO, or very large files, potentially causing memory exhaustion or a hung analysis run. Any matched `library "..." not found` strings from the unintended file are also propagated into output evidence, creating a limited information-disclosure channel.

title: Symlinkable temp root can delete global /dev/null
description: The helper builds its private execution root under /tmp/a90-v231-<pid>. /tmp is mounted world-writable, and mkdir_one() treats EEXIST as success without verifying that the existing path is a root-owned directory and not a symlink. This commit adds dev/null and sys/fs/selinux/null paths under that tree, and cleanup_paths() now unconditionally unlinks those paths whenever they are non-empty. A local attacker that can write to /tmp can pre-create likely /tmp/a90-v231-<future-pid>/root symlinks, for example pointing root to /. When the root-run helper later uses a matching PID, paths->dev_null resolves to /dev/null and cleanup_paths() unlinks the real /dev/null. With --null-device-mode enabled, materialize_null_devices() also performs unlink/mknod/chmod through the same symlinkable path. This can corrupt global device nodes or cause denial of service in the native init environment. The underlying predictable temporary-directory pattern existed before, but this commit introduced high-value device-node unlink/mknod operations under that path.

title: Unbounded output directory deletion in linker map tool
description: In analyze mode, the new wifi_linker_early_abort_map.py script resolves --out-dir and immediately passes it to reset_private_dir(). That helper only rejects a final-path symlink or non-directory, then calls shutil.rmtree(path). Because repo_path() preserves absolute paths, --out-dir can point outside the repository, and the deletion happens before validating the linker ELF or completing analysis setup. In a shared lab, CI wrapper, or other automation where an attacker can influence the output path, this can delete arbitrary directories writable by the script's user. The final-component symlink check also does not prevent escapes through symlinked parent directories.

title: Predictable temp linkerconfig allows root file clobber
description: The v232 helper now materializes /linkerconfig/ld.config.txt before running linker64. It builds the destination under /tmp/a90-v231-<pid>/root/linkerconfig, but /tmp is mounted mode=1777, the temp directory name is predictable from the process PID, and mkdir_one() treats EEXIST as success without verifying that the existing path is a directory owned by root with safe permissions. The new materialization path then unlinks and opens the destination with O_CREAT|O_APPEND or O_CREAT|O_TRUNC, without O_NOFOLLOW/O_EXCL and without using openat() from a trusted directory fd. A lower-privileged local process that can write to /tmp can pre-create candidate /tmp/a90-v231-* trees and race the ld.config.txt creation with a symlink to a root-owned target. In copy-real mode this can truncate and rewrite the target with data copied from the selected source; in minimal-vendor mode it can append the synthetic config. This can be used for local integrity/availability impact, and potentially persistence if writable helper/config targets are clobbered. The helper should use mkdtemp or another unpredictable private directory, fail on EEXIST, verify ownership/type/mode with lstat/fstat, and create ld.config.txt with openat(..., O_NOFOLLOW|O_CREAT|O_EXCL, ...) inside a verified root-owned directory.

title: Linker probe output can spoof safety-gate success
description: The commit adds a device helper that runs Android's linker and prints the child's stdout/stderr verbatim between A90_EXECNS_* markers. The host parser then parses those marker-delimited sections and classifies results. Two related issues make the probe's safety decision spoofable or misleading: first, raw child output is not escaped, so a malicious or malformed linker output stream containing A90_EXECNS_STDOUT_END, A90_EXECNS_END, or key=value lines can confuse the host parser. Second, even without delimiter injection, classify_execns_helper_result returns pass=True for output containing linkerconfig/namespace/dependency keywords before it checks child_signal or child_exit_code. A linker process that prints 'ld.config' or similar and then crashes with SIGSEGV is therefore accepted as android-linkerconfig-required or android-linker-list-runtime-gap. In this repository's workflow these manifests are used as safety gates for later privileged Wi-Fi/Android namespace work, so attacker-controlled device images or tampered Android runtime/linker output could cause false-positive automation evidence and move the operator past a failed probe.

title: CNSS runner executes before safety-gate decisions are enforced
description: The v229 runner is intended to fail closed unless prior v221-v228 safety decisions and the v228 allowlist/plan are valid. However, run_mode() only calculates prerequisite_checks and plan_checks, then immediately performs live device preflight and, in run mode with --allow-daemon-start --assume-yes, starts the daemon if preflight passes. The actual blocking logic is deferred to decide(), after the live actions have already occurred. As a result, missing, stale, or mismatched prerequisite manifests, or an unexpected v228 plan/allowlist, do not prevent the privileged root daemon start attempt; they only affect the final reported decision after the fact. This undermines the intended safety boundary around starting Android CNSS components from native init and can cause unsupported device state changes or require reboot recovery despite failed approval gates.

title: Live vendor export follows symlinks into sensitive paths
description: scripts/revalidation/wifi_vendor_live_export.py introduces a live device exporter that mounts sda29 read-only and pulls allowlisted vendor binaries/libraries by executing `/cache/bin/toybox base64` on paths under the temporary vendor mount. The path guard checks for absolute paths, `..`, and a few forbidden path components, but it does not verify that the mounted path is a regular file or that it is not a symlink. The script first records the full command output to `native/commands/...txt` via `capture_device()` and only later decodes and size-checks it. Therefore, if a vendor filesystem contains `bin/cnss-daemon`, `bin/cnss_diag`, or a discovered dependency as a symlink to `/data/misc/wifi/...`, `/cache/...`, `/proc/...`, or another sensitive/special file, the base64 command will follow the symlink and the contents can be written into host evidence captures even if the later size check rejects the pulled file. This violates the documented no-credential/no-arbitrary-device-read guardrail and can also cause hangs or large captures when symlinks point at special files.

title: Shim dry-run gate can pass with missing vendor evidence
description: The script treats absent JSON manifests as ordinary dictionaries with a missing flag, but the decision logic does not validate all required inputs. v221 is loaded and recorded but never passed into or checked by decide(). As a result, a run with a missing or failed v221 manifest can still return shim-dryrun-ready when v219/v223 are acceptable and v222 claims vendor-root-ready. Similarly, a missing v222 manifest is treated as a successful shim-source-required PASS rather than manual-review-required. This undermines the safety-gate semantics documented for v224: missing prerequisite evidence should stop the workflow, not produce a passing artifact. In this repository's threat model, false-positive automation success can weaken later Wi-Fi/daemon exposure gates and may allow unsafe follow-on experiments to proceed based on incomplete evidence.

title: Vendor ELF tool can escape vendor-root and read host files
description: The commit adds scripts/revalidation/wifi_vendor_elf_library_closure.py, which is intended to inspect only a host-visible vendor root. However, the target binary path is built from v218's vendor_relative_path without rejecting absolute paths or '..' components. In pathlib, vendor_root / '/bin/ls' ignores vendor_root entirely, and vendor_root / '../../...' can traverse out. resolve_existing_path() only enforces vendor-root containment for symlink targets; regular files are accepted without resolving and checking relative_to(vendor_root). The same issue exists for RPATH/RUNPATH resolution: map_android_path() accepts absolute non-/vendor paths and relative paths, and resolve_library() passes those candidates to the same unconstrained resolver. Once accepted, inspect_elf() invokes readelf and file_metadata() hashes the file. This permits attacker-controlled evidence/manifests or a malicious vendor ELF to probe arbitrary readable host files by path and leak their path, mode, size, and SHA-256 into the evidence output, and can also cause local DoS by hashing large files.

title: Untrusted manifest capture paths escape evidence directory
description: scripts/revalidation/wifi_service_replay_model.py reads capture files listed in the v206 manifest by doing `base / rel` and then `read_text()`. If a manifest or evidence bundle is attacker-controlled, `rel` can be absolute, contain `..`, or point through a symlink, causing the host tool to read files outside the evidence directory. Because there is no regular-file or size check, paths such as device nodes or very large files can also hang or exhaust memory. Parsed portions of the read content are then included in generated JSON/Markdown artifacts, so matching lines may be disclosed and malformed artifacts can cause host-side denial of service. This is introduced with the new script.

title: Wi-Fi lifecycle captures can leak unredacted identifiers
description: The added collector is documented as avoiding sensitive Wi-Fi collection, but live ADB mode captures Wi-Fi-filtered dmesg and logcat output and persists it to command files and manifest.json. The redaction function only masks MAC addresses and psk/password/passphrase/ssid/bssid values when they appear as key=value tokens. Android Wi-Fi logs commonly include network identifiers in other formats, such as quoted SSIDs, `SSID: ...`, supplicant/config-manager messages, or command metadata such as `adb -s <serial>`, which are not redacted before being written. If these evidence bundles are shared for debugging or reporting, they can disclose private Wi-Fi network names, device identifiers, and other operational metadata.

title: Symlinkable /tmp probe path can redirect root mount
description: native_vendor_asset_classifier.py builds its device-side probe directory as /tmp/a90-v210-<run-id>, where the default run-id is a timestamp and the optional run-id is only checked for characters, not for uniqueness or symlink safety. The command guard only checks string prefixes such as /tmp/a90-v210-... and then sends mkdir, mknodb, mount, and umount commands to the device root shell. On the target init, /tmp is mounted mode=1777. A lower-privileged local process on the device can pre-create or race a symlink such as /tmp/a90-v210-<predicted> -> / before the operator runs the classifier. The classifier will accept the path as 'under' the probe prefix, but the kernel will resolve the symlink when creating /sda29 and mounting on /vendor. This bypasses the documented guardrail that mounts should stay under the temporary probe path and can create root-owned device nodes or temporarily mount over persistent paths such as /vendor, causing integrity/availability impact. The tool should create a private, non-followed directory on the device side, verify lstat/realpath for each path before mutation, reject pre-existing paths/symlinks, and ideally use an unpredictable nonce.

title: Predictable /tmp probe path allows symlinked block mounts
description: The introduced script uses /tmp/a90-v209-<run-id> as the base for a temporary block device node and mountpoint. The default run-id is only a UTC timestamp to the second, and the user-supplied run-id permits predictable names. The command guard only checks that command arguments begin with that string; it does not verify on-device lstat/realpath properties or reject pre-existing paths. On the device, /tmp is mounted mode 1777, and the native mkdir and mknodb handlers treat EEXIST as success without checking whether the existing object is a directory, a symlink, or a block node with the expected major/minor. A local unprivileged process that can write to /tmp can pre-create /tmp/a90-v209-<predicted> as a symlink, or create a directory containing sda29/vendor symlinks, before the operator runs the probe. The script will then run root PID1 commands that may create a block node in an unintended directory, mount an attacker-selected existing block device through a symlink, or mount the vendor filesystem over an unintended target such as a symlink-resolved path outside the probe directory. This bypasses the script's documented guardrail that nodes and mounts stay under /tmp/a90-v209-* and can cause information exposure, persistent device-node creation, or availability impact by overmounting system paths.

title: V454 post-route steps inherit Wi-Fi secrets
description: V454 builds preflight/live shell scripts by importing the V453 prompt block, which reads the Wi-Fi SSID/PSK and exports them as A90_WIFI_SSID and A90_WIFI_PSK. After the V447 flow completes, V454 immediately runs V449, V450, and V452 post-route/proof commands before the EXIT trap cleanup runs. Because the variables are still exported, each post-route Python process inherits the raw Wi-Fi credentials even though those tools are documented as not needing or reading secrets. V450 also records env_state into its manifest/summary, leaking whether the SSID/PSK were present and their lengths. The raw PSK is not directly written by the shown code and evidence files are private-mode, so impact is limited, but this violates the intended secret-minimization guardrail and increases exposure to local same-user process-environment inspection, debugging/capture tooling, and persisted metadata leakage. The generated scripts should unset A90_WIFI_SSID and A90_WIFI_PSK before invoking V449/V450/V452, or invoke those commands with a sanitized environment.

title: Wi-Fi env secret lengths written without approval
description: The script claims Wi-Fi secret environment values are only read with explicit approval, but main() calls read_env_state() unconditionally. read_env_state() always fetches A90_WIFI_SSID and A90_WIFI_PSK and stores whether they are present plus their lengths. That env_state is then written into manifest.json and summary.md. As a result, simply running the harmless-looking `plan` mode, or running `run` without approval flags, can disclose credential metadata to evidence if the operator already has the Wi-Fi env vars set. This does not expose the raw SSID/PSK, and evidence files are private by default, so impact is limited, but it violates the intended approval boundary and leaks sensitive metadata useful for credential guessing or accidental sharing.

title: Wi-Fi env approval bypass leaks secret metadata
description: The script claims Wi-Fi environment values are read only after both approval flags are supplied, but main() calls read_env_present() when the command is plan or when approval is missing. read_env_present() retrieves A90_WIFI_SSID and A90_WIFI_PSK with os.environ.get() and stores exact lengths. That env_state is then embedded into manifest.json and summary.md. This does not expose raw SSID or PSK values, and the evidence directory is intended to be private, but it still bypasses the approval gate and leaks sensitive metadata about configured Wi-Fi secrets without consent.

title: Wi-Fi policy target id can leak raw network secrets
description: The validator is intended to keep raw SSID, BSSID, passphrase, password, and PSK values out of policy evidence. However, it allows any target id matching a broad alphanumeric/dot/dash/underscore pattern and then writes that id into both manifest.json and summary.md. Common SSIDs and some PSKs satisfy this pattern. A private policy using the real SSID or a credential-like value as the target id will still validate as v442-wifi-target-policy-allowlist-ready, and the raw value will be persisted in evidence despite the script reporting that the policy contains no raw credentials or network identifiers. The raw-field regex only detects forbidden JSON key names and MAC-looking strings; it does not prevent secret values from being placed in fields that are later copied to evidence.

title: V433 evidence redaction misses IPv6 addresses
description: The commit adds a V433 collector that captures `ip route`, `ip route get`, `ip -o addr show wlan0`, `dumpsys connectivity`, and `ss -lntu` output. Those sources commonly contain IPv6 global, ULA, link-local, gateway, and DNS addresses. Captured output is persisted after passing through `redact_text`, and selected lines are again included in the manifest/summary through `matching_lines`. However, `redact_text` masks MAC addresses and IPv4 dotted quads only; it has no IPv6 redaction. As a result, generated evidence can disclose lab network addressing and device IPv6 identifiers. Evidence directories are private by default, so this is not a remote compromise, but it is still an introduced information disclosure in the new V433 workflow, especially if evidence bundles are shared in reports or committed accidentally.

title: V421 plan gate can be fooled by crafted manifests
description: The commit adds `scripts/revalidation/wifi_v421_micro_hwservice_query_plan.py`, which is intended to be a fail-closed gate before implementing a future privileged helper/runner. However, its upstream evidence checks are not strict. For V420 and V411, the script checks `v420.get("pass")` and `v411_query.get("pass")` directly, so JSON values such as `"false"` are treated as truthy and pass the gate. For V414, the check does not validate the `pass` field at all; any manifest with decision `v414-static-runtime-targets-ready` and at least one runtime pattern is accepted. In addition, `runtime_patterns()` only checks that a pattern contains `::` and `/`, rather than enforcing the documented Samsung `vendor.samsung.hardware.wifi@2.x::ISehWifi/default` allowlist. If an attacker can poison local evidence files or cause automation to run this script with attacker-controlled manifest paths, the script can produce a ready decision and exit 0 for out-of-scope or failed evidence. The immediate impact is limited because this script itself does not execute device commands, but it weakens the approval chain for later privileged device-side actions.

title: V411 linter can falsely pass malformed command manifests
description: The added host-only linter is intended to prove that the V411 workflow is limited to `/system/bin/lshal list --types=binderized --neat` before deploy or live query work proceeds. However, it does not validate the approved command structure or argument relationships. It only checks that `--mode` appears somewhere in the command list, that the expected mode string appears somewhere in the same list, and that `--allow-hal-service-query` appears somewhere. A command can therefore pass while the actual value following `--mode` is a different, broader mode, as long as the expected binderized mode token is present as an unrelated argument. The linter also trusts top-level manifest fields such as `device_commands_executed` and `wifi_bringup_executed` instead of deriving them from the command or regenerating the plan. I verified locally that a crafted approved-plan manifest with `--mode wifi-hal-composite-lshal-list` and the binderized mode string placed elsewhere still produces `v411-binderized-lshal-contract-pass`. This is not direct code execution, but it undermines a security/safety gate that operators may use to approve root device operations.

title: lshal query timeout can be bypassed by inherited pipes
description: The commit adds run_lshal_service_query_child() with an intended 3000 ms bound. However, the polling loop continues while stdout_open or stderr_open is true, and the timeout/kill logic only runs while !child_done. If /system/bin/lshal, a wrapper, or a spawned descendant exits the direct child but leaves either inherited pipe fd open, child_done becomes true and the loop will keep polling forever with no further timeout enforcement or process-group kill. This defeats the helper's bounded-query safety property and can leave the on-device privileged helper/native shell command stuck until manual cleanup or reboot. This is primarily an availability issue and requires unusual or malicious lshal behavior/system image control, so impact is limited.

title: V407 runner allows unapproved legacy Wi-Fi HAL target
description: The V407 approval plan restricts the live operation to starting servicemanager, hwservicemanager, and the first Wi-Fi HAL candidate vendor.wifi_hal_ext. However, the new runner does not enforce that target. It imports the V405 runner and reuses its argparse configuration, where --target-profile accepts both vendor-wifi-hal-ext and vendor-wifi-hal-legacy. The V407 build_helper_argv then forwards args.target_profile directly into the root helper command. As a result, a run with the exact V407 approval phrase plus --target-profile vendor-wifi-hal-legacy will still pass the V407 approval gate and construct a live command for the legacy android.hardware.wifi@1.0-service target, which is outside the documented approval scope. This is a safety/authorization gate bypass for a privileged device operation, though exploitation requires local control of the host-side runner arguments and the exact approval flow.

title: Stale proof manifests can falsely approve SELinuxfs mount
description: wifi_selinuxfs_mount_approval_packet.py runs the V397 SELinux surface proof and then loads the proof manifest from the output directory, but the proof validation path does not require that the subprocess completed successfully. If the proof subprocess times out or crashes before overwriting an existing manifest, build_checks() still trusts the old manifest decision/pass/check details. Because the default output directory is reusable and --out-dir can point at an existing directory, stale or pre-seeded evidence can cause the packet to return selinuxfs-mount-approval-packet-ready and emit approved V399 commands without a fresh device proof. This does not bypass the live executor's separate approval phrase, and it does not directly execute arbitrary commands, but it weakens the intended safety gate for privileged device mutation.

title: Preapproval audit trusts stale executor manifests
description: The audit records whether the executor plan and no-approval subprocesses succeeded, but it does not use those results when deciding whether the checks passed. Instead, it loads manifest.json from the executor output directories and validates only the manifest contents. Because the output directory is stable and user-controlled, an old or pre-created manifest can satisfy command_false_manifest_ok() even if the current executor invocation fails, times out, or never writes a fresh manifest. This can cause the preapproval audit to report that the approval gate was verified when it was not actually exercised in the current run.

title: Binder devnodes are left behind after helper cleanup
description: The new service-manager-start-only path materializes /dev/binder, /dev/hwbinder, and /dev/vndbinder as world-readable/writable character devices under the helper temporary root. However, cleanup_paths() was not updated to unlink these new paths. It only removes existing dev_null and then attempts to rmdir(paths->dev). Because the Binder nodes remain in that directory, rmdir(paths->dev), then rmdir(paths->root), and rmdir(paths->base) fail, leaving the temporary tree behind. In the normal case the parent base directory is intended to be 0700 root-owned, which limits practical exposure, but this still breaks the stated private/temporary property of the namespace and can leave usable Binder device nodes reachable to any process that has retained access to the chroot/tree or if the temp directory creation is influenced by local filesystem state. Since these are Binder kernel device entry points created with mode 0666, the omission is security-relevant even though exploitation is local and constrained.

title: Dashboard can pass with missing blocker evidence
description: The dashboard is intended to fail closed when required evidence is missing. However, item_from_manifest() applies status_override before checking whether load_manifest() returned present=false. Several required tracks pass status_override="blocked". If those fixed-path manifests are absent, they are recorded as present=false but status="blocked", not status="missing". decide() only treats status="missing" as incomplete, so the dashboard can return decision wifi-readiness-dashboard-ready-blocked-by-v317 and pass=true despite absent blocker evidence such as the v317 refusal proof or service-manager prerequisite evidence. This weakens the integrity of the approval/readiness gate and can mislead later privileged Wi-Fi/property-runtime work, although the script itself does not execute device commands.

title: Cleanup bypasses refreshed approval blocker
description: This commit adds an approval-refresh manifest check intended to require the latest V327 approval evidence before live run/cleanup actions. However, build_manifest only applies blocker checks to the `run` path. The `cleanup` path still calls `cleanup_sequence()` whenever `approval_ok(args, v316)` is true, without checking whether the new `approval-refresh` blocker, or any other blocker, passed. As a result, invoking `cleanup` with the old static approval phrase and mutation flags can still send `run /cache/bin/toybox rm -rf /mnt/sdext/a90/private-property-v317` to the rooted device even when the refreshed approval manifest is missing or invalid. The impact is limited because the command is a fixed workdir cleanup and the operator must already have access to the device control channel, but it violates the fail-closed approval-refresh gate added by this commit.

title: Property root allowlist follows intermediate symlinks
description: The commit adds a read-only property lookup helper that is intended to bind only a private `/mnt/sdext/a90/private-property-v317/.../dev/__properties__` tree into the helper chroot. The allowlist check only verifies the string prefix/suffix and rejects `..`. Later, `materialize_private_properties()` calls `lstat()` on the full path, which detects a symlink only if the final component itself is a symlink; Linux still follows symlinks in intermediate path components. As a result, a path such as `/mnt/sdext/a90/private-property-v317/link/dev/__properties__`, where `link` points to `/`, passes the textual allowlist and the final-component `lstat()` check, but `mount()` binds the real global `/dev/__properties__` into the private namespace. This violates the intended isolation and can cause the proof/helper to read from the live/global property area instead of the approved private copy. The practical impact is limited because the helper only runs `/system/bin/getprop` for a narrow read-only property-key allowlist and requires the attacker to influence the device-side property root/workdir or helper invocation, but it is still a newly introduced namespace escape from the stated security boundary.

title: Transfer estimate undercounts manifest upload size
description: The commit adds a transfer-plan blocker intended to show and gate expected serial command volume before live device mutation. File payload chunks are estimated from actual local file sizes, but the final manifest upload is estimated as `4096 + len(files) * 512` bytes. During live execution, however, the manifest is generated from `asdict(entry)` for every file and includes attacker-influenced `relative_path`, `local_path`, and `remote_path` strings. Since those strings are not bounded to 512 bytes per file by the estimator, a crafted v312 layout with many valid small files and long allowed paths can make `estimated_commands` stay below the 5000-command cutoff while the real manifest upload requires many more base64 chunks and device commands. This does not bypass path validation or provide code execution, but it weakens the newly introduced approval/safety control and can cause an unexpectedly long root-device serial session or availability degradation after operator approval.

title: Property mapping proof can pass with no mapped properties
description: The script is intended to prove that selected Android-backed seed properties map through captured `property_contexts` before later runtime layout work. However, `build_manifest()` never requires the seed file to be present or `selected_properties(seed)` to return at least one property. If a v295 manifest supplies at least 100 context rules, the `selected-key-mapping` check sees an empty `missing` list and passes, while `roundtrip_ok` remains `True` because the roundtrip loop has no mappings to check. The v295/v310 prerequisite checks are warnings, not blockers, so the final decision can become `property-context-mapping-ready` with `mappings: []`. This undermines the validation gate and could cause later privileged/runtime packaging steps to trust an empty or unproven property mapping artifact.

title: Seed generation runs before live handoff validation
description: In `android_capture_postprocess.py`, `build_manifest()` loads the v300 live manifest but runs `run_seed()` whenever v297 has decision `android-property-capture-pass` and v298 has decision `property-baseline-compare-ready`. The stricter checks that v300 is present, has decision `android-capture-executor-pass`, has a truthy pass flag, and includes an ok `restore-native` step are only performed later inside `decide()`. As a result, stale or attacker-supplied v297/v298 manifests can cause a v301 seed manifest to be generated even when the v300 live handoff is missing or failed. The script may then return a waiting/live-failed postprocess decision, but the misleading v301 seed artifact has already been written and could be consumed by later tooling or manual review as Android-backed seed evidence.

title: Incomplete redaction leaks Android identifiers in evidence
description: The commit adds a host-side evidence collector that captures ADB output and Android properties and stores them under the selected evidence directory. Although it attempts to redact serials, MACs, SSIDs, BSSIDs, and password-like values, the regexes miss common output formats. For example, an ADB failure such as `error: device 'R58M123ABC' not found` is transformed into `<adb-serial> device 'R58M123ABC' not found`, leaving the actual serial visible. Android getprop bracket syntax such as `[persist.wifi.ssid]: [HomeWiFi]`, `[persist.vendor.wifi.password]: [secret]`, or serial-like keys other than `ro.serialno` and `ro.boot.serialno` are also not covered. The resulting text is written to `commands/*.txt` and embedded in the manifest capture records. The evidence helpers use private permissions and tmp output is gitignored, which limits impact, but the tool's stated privacy guardrail is not met and shared evidence bundles can disclose device identifiers or Wi-Fi-related sensitive values.

title: Malformed ELF symbol table can hang extractor
description: The added ELF parser computes the number of symbols as `section.sh_size // section.sh_entsize` using attacker-controlled section header fields, then loops over that count. When an entry offset is outside the actual file, the code uses `continue` rather than stopping or rejecting the malformed section. As a result, a malicious vendor ELF with a huge `sh_size` and small `sh_entsize` can make the script consume CPU indefinitely even if the file itself is tiny. This affects the host-side validation workflow when run on untrusted or attacker-supplied vendor evidence.

title: CNSS inventory PASS ignores failed safety checks
description: The commit adds a host-side inventory script intended to gate future bounded CNSS daemon start planning. It records several safety-relevant checks, including the native init version, CNSS daemon NET_ADMIN/group contract, and v241 linker result, but classify() only evaluates runtime checks whose names start with live-. As a result, non-live runtime checks can fail while the script still returns cnss-runtime-inventory-ready-for-launcher-contract-plan. The --expect-version option is also not actually honored: the script hard-codes v159 in build_runtime_checks(), then classify() ignores that check anyway. Additionally, the v218 prerequisite check only requires a daemons array and does not require the prior manifest to have passed or to have the expected decision. This weakens the safety gate for later privileged CNSS launcher work and could let stale, failed, or tampered evidence advance the workflow.

title: Unbounded ADB output capture can exhaust host resources
description: android_linkerconfig_capture.py treats ADB output as trusted and uses subprocess.run(..., stdout=PIPE, stderr=PIPE) for every device command. capture_command() and capture_file() then decode and write the complete output. The fixed capture targets are intended to be small Android text files, but the data source is across the ADB trust boundary. A malicious attached device, compromised adb server, or unexpected symlink/special file exposed at /linkerconfig/ld.config.txt could stream very large data before the timeout, causing Python to buffer it in memory and potentially crash the host process. If the command exits, the script can also write arbitrarily large evidence files. The timeout limits duration but not bytes, and TimeoutExpired is not handled gracefully.

title: Wi-Fi gate reports PASS while security warnings remain
description: The newly added wifi_exposure_security_gate_v3.py classifies missing or incomplete root-control exposure evidence, credential-denial evidence, and changed/missing v220 active-Wi-Fi state as warnings. However, decide() checks for blocked items before warnings and returns pass_ok=True for the still-no-go state whenever any blocker exists. main() then prints PASS and exits 0. As a result, a run with missing exposure reports, missing v220 evidence, or missing credential-policy evidence can still be treated by automation as a successful v225 gate as long as vendor_evidence or shim_materialization remains blocked. The decision text remains still-no-go, so this does not directly enable Wi-Fi in this commit, but it weakens the security gate by allowing incomplete security evidence to be recorded as a passing run.

title: Unredacted native captures are embedded in v217 manifests
description: In collect_native(), the script writes a redacted copy of each native command output to native/commands/*.txt, but then calls capture_to_manifest(capture) on the original CommandCapture and appends that unredacted data to the captures list. main() later writes this captures list into manifest.json. The command list includes /cache/bin/toybox dmesg and /sys/kernel/debug traversal, so the manifest can contain unredacted kernel log excerpts, boot parameters, serial-like identifiers not covered by the narrow redaction, and debug path information. This does not create remote code execution and the EvidenceStore writes private files by default, but it defeats the script's own redaction boundary and becomes a data exposure risk when evidence bundles are copied, attached to reports, or collected in a shared lab/CI workflow.

title: Predictable /tmp file in ICNSS helper build script
description: scripts/revalidation/build_icnssctl_helper.sh uses the predictable filename /tmp/a90_icnssctl_dynamic_check.txt for temporary readelf output. Because the file is opened with shell redirection, an attacker on the same host who can pre-create that path may be able to make the script follow a symlink and truncate a file writable by the script runner, or use a FIFO/special file to block the build. This is a local, host-side issue requiring the victim/operator to run the build script. The script should use mktemp in a private directory, clean it up with trap, and avoid following attacker-controlled paths.

title: Predictable /tmp file allows build-script file clobber
description: scripts/revalidation/build_fwpathctl_helper.sh uses the predictable world-writable path /tmp/a90_fwpathctl_dynamic_check.txt for an output redirection. Shell redirection follows symlinks, so a local attacker on the same host can pre-create that path as a symlink to any file writable by the user running the script. When the operator runs the build helper, the target file is truncated and overwritten with readelf output. In the normal workflow this is a local build-time issue and the script does not require sudo, so impact is limited; however, if the script is run with elevated privileges, the clobber target could be more sensitive. The script should use mktemp or a private repository-local temporary file and clean it up with a trap.

title: Failed ADB output can forge Wi-Fi baseline PASS
description: The collector stores stdout/stderr from an ADB shell command even when the command exits non-zero, then classification reads every capture without checking capture.ok. The final PASS condition only requires ok_capture_count > 0, and adb_wait is included in that count. As a result, if adb wait-for-device succeeds but all shell commands fail, failed command output can still populate interface/kernel evidence and produce a PASS exit status. A malicious or broken adb wrapper/server/device can print strings such as wlan0 in an error path and cause the script to report ready-for-readonly-nl80211-probe-plan despite no successful evidence collection. This is an integrity flaw in a security validation workflow rather than a direct code execution issue.

title: V410 linter crashes with default approved-plan path
description: The new scripts/revalidation/wifi_v410_arg_budget_linter.py hard-codes DEFAULT_APPROVED_PLAN under tmp/wifi. The repository ignores tmp/, so this manifest is not versioned and is absent in a clean checkout. Because --approved-plan defaults to that path and main() unconditionally calls load_json() on it, the script crashes before producing a controlled blocked manifest. It also creates the output directory before failing, leaving partial evidence directories. This is a functional availability/reliability bug in the newly added linter, not a security vulnerability.

title: Unapproved V382 executor paths crash during summary render
description: render_summary now unconditionally reads manifest["planned_live_preflight_command"]. base_result includes that field, but refusal_manifest, which is returned for approval-blocked deploy, live, and full commands, was not updated. main always calls render_summary(manifest), so the fail-closed refusal path crashes after writing manifest.json instead of producing summary/output. I confirmed this with `python3 scripts/revalidation/wifi_v382_deploy_live_executor.py --out-dir tmp/test-v382-exec-noapproval deploy`, which exits with KeyError: 'planned_live_preflight_command'. This does not appear to execute device commands or bypass approval, but it is an introduced availability/regression bug in a safety-gate workflow.

title: V382 router can route its own previous output
description: The wrapper sets DEFAULT_OUT_DIR to tmp/wifi/v382-service-manager-start-only-result-router and DEFAULT_V376_GLOB to v382-*/manifest.json. The shared router's default route path searches tmp/wifi with that glob and then writes manifest.json into the output directory. Because the output directory itself starts with v382-, the generated router manifest matches the input glob. Running the V382 router once with defaults creates tmp/wifi/v382-service-manager-start-only-result-router/manifest.json; running it again with defaults can load that router output as if it were the live result manifest, producing an incorrect manual-review route. This is a fail-closed workflow correctness issue rather than a direct security vulnerability, because it does not execute device commands or mutate the device.

title: False PASS recorded for V359 blocker snapshot
description: This commit records V359 as a completed clean-head PASS and states that only the exact V317 approval phrase remains as a blocker. I ran the referenced command, `python3 scripts/revalidation/wifi_v317_blocker_snapshot.py --out-dir tmp/audit-v359-snapshot-check snapshot`, from the checked-out commit. It returned `v317-live-blocker-snapshot-blocked` with failed checks including `v357-audit-rc`, `v357-awaiting-approval`, `v357-approval-blocker-only`, and `v350-checklist-ready`. The change therefore introduces inaccurate operational/readiness documentation. This is not a direct code-execution or authentication vulnerability because no runtime, flashing, native init, or executor code changed, and the V351 executor still refreshes and validates readiness before live device mutation. However, the false PASS could mislead an operator reviewing the plan/report into believing the live gate is ready when the repository evidence does not currently support that.

title: Unreproducible readiness PASS recorded
description: The patch does not modify executable code, device runtime logic, flashing logic, or network-facing services. However, it does introduce a documentation/evidence integrity bug: the report and task queue now state that the V317 final readiness aggregator passed on clean HEAD and that the only remaining blocker is the exact V317 approval phrase. In this checkout, running the exact referenced command on clean HEAD returned `v317-final-readiness-blocked`, with blocked steps `v344-gate-refresh` and `v348-command-contract`. This could mislead an operator reviewing the plan/report into believing readiness evidence is complete when the checked-out repository cannot currently reproduce that state. The actual scripts still fail closed, so this is not an independently exploitable security vulnerability.

title: Selftest crashes on missing or malformed plan manifests
description: The new `selftest` subcommand loads the plan manifest and immediately mutates `bad_path["files"][0]["remote_path"]` and `bad_transfer["transfer_estimate"]["estimated_commands"]`. If the default manifest has not been generated, or if a malformed/partial manifest is supplied, `load_json()` returns a minimal dictionary without `files` or `transfer_estimate`, causing the selftest to terminate with an uncaught exception. This is reproducible on a fresh checkout by running `python3 scripts/revalidation/wifi_private_property_namespace_proof_audit.py --out-dir /tmp/audit-test selftest`, which raises `KeyError: 'files'`. The normal audit path is not bypassed, and no privileged device command execution is introduced, but the new selftest is not fail-closed/robust as documented.

title: Serializer proof can crash on long seed values
description: The newly added wifi_property_serializer_proof.py filters seed entries only by state, ro.* prefix, and non-empty value. It does not enforce the same value-length validation used by earlier property proof tooling before passing entries into PropAreaBuilder. PropAreaBuilder.create_prop_info() rejects values whose UTF-8 length is >= 92 bytes by raising ValueError, and build_manifest() does not catch that exception or convert it into a blocked proof decision. A malformed, unexpected, or user-supplied seed JSON containing a ready ro.* property with a long value causes the tool to terminate with a traceback instead of writing a complete manifest/report. This is a host-side robustness/availability bug in the new proof script, not a direct device compromise.

title: Hard-coded ignored v295 evidence path breaks default run
description: The newly added host-side strategy model defaults --v295-manifest to tmp/wifi/v295-property-snapshot-live-20260519-142740/manifest.json. The tmp/ directory is git-ignored, and the documented validation command only supplies --out-dir, not --v295-manifest. In the provided checkout, running the documented command causes load_manifest() to mark the input as absent, choose_decision() to return property-shim-input-missing, and main() to exit nonzero. This does not create a security boundary bypass or expose secrets, but it makes the committed validation/report claims non-reproducible unless the operator happens to have the author's local timestamped evidence directory.

title: Focused sampler can pass with failed focused captures
description: The new sampler marks every focused `run ...` observation command with `expect_ok=False`, then computes signals regardless of whether each capture succeeded. Its pass condition for having observed the serial run only requires any capture in a during-sample to be OK. Because unauthenticated/non-focused commands such as `ping` or `status` can succeed while all focused `run` commands fail, the tool can classify the run as `icnss-qca6390-focused-no-during-delta` without actually collecting valid focused state. This is a validation correctness bug: it can produce false PASS evidence, but it does not directly create an exploitable security boundary bypass.

title: No security vulnerability introduced
description: The new script sends fixed, mostly read-only native-init commands through the existing a90ctl cmdv1 transport, writes evidence through the existing private EvidenceStore helper, and does not add a new authentication boundary, network listener, partition writer, shell-string execution path, or unsafe archive/parser surface. One minor functional issue exists: the documented `icnss-wlan-readiness-visible` outcome is effectively unreachable because WLAN readiness is treated as a critical check failure before classification reaches that branch. This is not security-impacting.

title: Offset mismatch check is bypassed by default argument
description: The script parses v236 crash evidence and computes offsets, then intends to fail if the computed offset does not match the expected value. However, `--offset` is defined with a non-empty default string (`0x1002f4`). In `analyze_command()`, `if args.offset:` is therefore always true, even when the user did not pass `--offset`, and `selected_offset` is overwritten with `expected_offset`. As a result, a consistent but unexpected computed offset is reported as the expected offset and the `linker-offset-unexpected` branch is effectively unreachable. This is primarily an evidence-integrity/correctness issue rather than a direct security vulnerability.
